caseName: The State of Western Australia v Raven [No 2]
outcome: Respondent declared a high risk serious offender; supervision order made for 3 years from 29/06/2026; no continuing detention order. Curfew condition and associate-disclosure condition removed as unnecessary.
weight: Single-judge first-instance decision of the Supreme Court applying settled HRSO principles; expressly turns on its own facts.
oneLine: WHITBY J declared serial fire-setter Victor RAVEN a high risk serious offender under the High Risk Serious Offenders Act 2020 (WA) and made a 3-year supervision order rather than continuing detention.
whatHappened: The State applied on 11/11/2025 for a restriction order against Victor Redvers RAVEN, then serving 3 years for wilfully lighting a fire likely to injure or damage (s 32(2)(a) Bush Fires Act 1954 (WA)) - he tied a match to an incense stick and left it in dried undergrowth behind Albany Senior High School. RAVEN, 69, has a mild intellectual disability, paranoid personality disorder and autism spectrum disorder, and a fire-setting history stretching back to 1972, much of it linked to a long-standing grievance against Albany Senior High School. Two experts (Professor PYSZORA and Dr YEWERS) assessed him as a high risk of further fire-setting if not supervised. Both the State and RAVEN's counsel supported a supervision order over continuing detention.
whatHeld: WHITBY J was satisfied to a high degree of probability that RAVEN is a high risk serious offender: he poses an unacceptable risk of committing a serious fire-setting offence, the potential harm being catastrophic. A restriction order was necessary because NDIS funding and stable accommodation with his brother were not enough to manage the risk. The court chose a supervision order as the least invasive option that still adequately protects the community, being satisfied RAVEN would substantially comply (he had complied with the interim order). A 3-year supervision order was imposed with conditions including electronic monitoring, exclusion from Albany and bushland (except his Kauring block), and prohibitions on possessing fire-starting implements.
whatItMeans: This is an outcome under the post-sentence HRSO regime, not a guide to investigation, but it is useful background. It shows how a persistent fire-setting offending pattern, combined with cogent expert risk assessment, supports a finding of unacceptable risk even where an offender has complied with interim supervision. For your casework, note the practical conditions imposed - electronic monitoring, police search of residence/vehicle/person (on request, to monitor compliance), exclusion zones, and bans on fire-starting implements and flammable substances - which are the kind of conditions you may be enforcing if you encounter an HRSO subject. The judgment also stresses information sharing between agencies and police to detect new grievances before they escalate.
verdict: AWARENESS only - this is a post-sentence civil restriction decision that does not change how you investigate, gather evidence or charge.

caseName: Cullen v The State of New South Wales
appealFrom: NSW Court of Appeal
outcome: Appeal dismissed — State not liable
weight: Persuasive principle (HCA); statute is NSW
oneLine: Police owe bystanders a duty of care when they move into a crowd to deal with a public-safety threat — but a proportionate response to a real threat is not negligence, even when someone gets hurt.
whatHappened: On 26 January 2017, an “Invasion Day” protest march of about 5,000 people moved through inner Sydney. CULLEN was watching from the crowd. As officers of the Operational Services Group (OSG) moved in — and while Leading Senior Constable Livermore was arresting a marcher — a melee erupted and CULLEN was knocked to the ground and seriously injured. At trial she was awarded $800,000; the NSW Court of Appeal reversed that. She appealed to the High Court.
whatHeld: The appeal turned on three questions — duty, breach, and scope. Duty — yes: when intervening in the march, the officers owed a duty to avoid physical injury to a class of people that included bystanders foreseeably at risk from the operation. Breach — no: their actions were not unreasonable given the public-safety threat they were responding to at the time. The six-judge joint reasons held that, with no breach, the scope question did not arise; Edelman J, agreeing there was no breach, would have decided scope and held the duty did extend to the appellant. Appeal dismissed (unanimous on the result).
whatItMeans: Read it for the principle, not the charge — this is NSW civil negligence, not WA criminal law, so it changes no offence you lay. But the principle is High Court and it travels: when you intervene operationally (a crowd, a public-order job, an arrest in a tight space) you owe bystanders a duty of care, and you are judged on the reasonableness of your response to the threat as it appeared at the time, not with hindsight. What carried the day was that a decisive response to a real, urgent threat was within the range of reasonable responses — judged in the agony of the moment, not with hindsight. Still, record your threat assessment and why the response was proportionate (running sheet, notes, BWC). WA's Civil Liability Act 2002 has broadly similar breach provisions, but this decision turns on the NSW Act — treat the principle as persuasive, not as authority on WA legislation.
verdict: Useful for use-of-force and civil-liability exposure. It does not change how you investigate, gather evidence, or lay a charge.

court: Supreme Court of WA — Court of Appeal
appealFrom: Supreme Court of WA
outcome: Appeal dismissed — but on the facts (the schizophrenia was not shown to operate independently of the cannabis), NOT because voluntary intoxication is an absolute bar to ss 27/28.
weight: WA Court of Appeal — binds WA courts below
oneLine: Voluntary intoxication does NOT automatically defeat an unsoundness-of-mind defence: an accused can still rely on s 27 if a mental impairment deprived them of capacity independently of the intoxication — but not where the intoxication itself (or its effect on a condition) caused it. STEFANSKI lost only because his schizophrenia couldn't be separated from his cannabis use.
whatHappened: An appeal against conviction on guilty pleas to two counts of murder. The appellant argued he could not in law be guilty (or was not guilty by reason of unsoundness of mind) where a psychosis had been triggered by his voluntary cannabis use — turning on the construction of ss 27, 28(1) and 28(2) of the Criminal Code (WA). The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal. (Your instructor placed the facts at a ‘Greenwood’ murder; the judgment does not name Greenwood — the legal point and citation are what matter.)
whatHeld: The Court of Appeal (Buss P, Mazza JA, Beech JA) construed ss 27, 28(1) and 28(2) of the Criminal Code (WA) and held — rejecting the contrary view in Herbert as 'wrong' — that an accused who intentionally became intoxicated may still rely on s 27(1) if they prove, on the balance of probabilities, a 'mental impairment' that deprived them of a relevant capacity independently of and without regard to the intoxication. What s 28(2) bars is reliance on s 27 where the voluntary intoxication itself — or its effect in triggering or worsening a pre-existing condition — caused the loss of capacity. The appeal was dismissed on the facts: the psychiatric evidence could not show STEFANSKI's paranoid schizophrenia operated independently of his admitted cannabis intoxication, and his guilty pleas were a considered free choice (Meissner).
whatItMeans: The practical lesson survives, but the rule is narrower than the shorthand. Get bloods early — not because a voluntary intoxicant automatically defeats an insanity defence (it does not), but because proving what was in the system goes to the question that actually decides these cases: could the mental impairment have operated independently of the intoxicant? For any serious matter, secure a forensic blood sample before alcohol/cannabis/meth metabolises, so the psychiatric picture can be disentangled. Exam heads-up: the simplified line 'voluntary intoxication can't ground unsoundness of mind' overstates it — that is not what the Court held.
verdict: ACTION — drives an investigative step (early forensic bloods) and corrects a common overstatement of the intoxication/insanity rule.

court: Supreme Court of WA — Court of Appeal
caseName: Gibson v The State of Western Australia
appealFrom: Supreme Court of WA
outcome: Appeal allowed; manslaughter conviction (on a guilty plea) set aside; judgment of acquittal entered — no retrial. (Orders made 12/04/2017; the Court's reasons, [2017] WASCA 141, were published 28/07/2017.)
weight: WA Court of Appeal — binds WA courts below
oneLine: GIBSON — a Pintupi man with significant cognitive impairment and very little English — pleaded guilty to a manslaughter he likely never understood he could fight. The plea lacked integrity, so the conviction was set aside and a judgment of acquittal entered.
whatHappened: Joshua WARNEKE was found dead beside Old Broome Road in February 2010. GIBSON — an 18-year-old Pintupi man from Kiwirrkurra, unable to read English and with significant cognitive impairment — was charged with murder in 2012. His records of interview were excluded at first instance (not voluntary; Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA) breaches; an interpreter plainly required). With the interviews gone, the case rested on companion witnesses whose accounts kept shifting. Instructions to plead were taken at two rushed conferences — one with no interpreter, one with an interpreter not accredited in his language — and GIBSON pleaded guilty to manslaughter, receiving 7 years 6 months. Official reviews later found the inculpatory witness statements had falsely implicated him.
whatHeld: The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal under s 30(3)(c) of the Criminal Appeals Act 2004 (WA): a conviction founded on a guilty plea will be set aside as a miscarriage of justice where the integrity of the plea is impugned — here because GIBSON's significant cognitive impairment and profound language disability meant he did not sufficiently understand the plea or the real choice between pleading guilty and standing trial (where an acquittal would mean release). The recognised categories for overturning a guilty plea are not closed (Vella), and the rule that a plea is a free choice by a person 'of sound mind and understanding' (Meissner) presupposes an understanding he did not have. Because the only inculpatory witnesses were now unreliable and could not properly be led — leaving no case to answer — the Court entered a judgment of acquittal rather than ordering a retrial (s 30(5)(b)).
whatItMeans: Your lessons sit in what went wrong, not the appeal point. Get the interview right: GIBSON's records of interview were thrown out for involuntariness and Criminal Investigation Act breaches, and once they were gone the case had no spine. For a vulnerable or Aboriginal suspect, use a properly accredited interpreter in the right language and a prisoner's friend, and explain the caution phrase by phrase — an untrained community member standing in as interpreter was not enough (read with Anunga). And don't shape your witnesses: the case collapsed because companion accounts shifted and 'critical details' had been introduced by interviewers — keep questions non-leading, corroborate independently, and keep investigating beyond the admission. (The 'reasonable suspicion / suspect status' point you may link to 'Gibson' is actually the separate first-instance ruling that excluded the interviews — The State of WA v Gibson [2014] WASC 240 — not this appeal.)
verdict: ACTION — a wrongful-conviction cautionary tale that drives interview discipline: lawful, voluntary, properly-interpreted records of interview, and witness evidence you have not contaminated.

appealFrom: Court of Appeal of the Supreme Court of Queensland
outcome: Crown appeal allowed; orders of the Queensland Court of Appeal set aside; appeal to that Court dismissed; jury's murder conviction restored.
weight: Unanimous decision of five Justices of the High Court of Australia; binding and authoritative nationally on circumstantial evidence, intent, post-offence conduct and the review of "unreasonable verdict" appeals.
oneLine: The High Court restored Gerard BADEN-CLAY's murder conviction, holding that a hypothesis of unintentional killing not raised at trial and contradicted by his own evidence could not render the jury's verdict unreasonable.
whatHappened: Allison BADEN-CLAY disappeared from her home overnight on 19/20 April 2012; her body was found on 30/04/2012 under a bridge at Kholo Creek, about 13km away. The respondent had been in a long-running affair with Ms McHugh, had promised to leave his wife by 1 July, and faced the prospect of both women attending the same conference the next day. He had fingernail-type scratches on his cheek (which he claimed were shaving cuts), gave false accounts to police about the affair, and told Ms McHugh to "lie low." Leaves from his home were found on the body and the deceased's blood was in her car. He gave evidence at trial denying any involvement in her death. A Queensland jury convicted him of murder on 15/07/2014. The Queensland Court of Appeal substituted a manslaughter verdict, finding the prosecution had not excluded a hypothesis (raised for the first time on appeal) that he struck her in a struggle and she died from a fall without intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm. The Crown appealed to the High Court.
whatHeld: The appeal was allowed and the murder conviction restored. The Court held the hypothesis relied on by the Court of Appeal was not open on the evidence — it was mere speculation, contrary to the accused's own sworn evidence (which placed him absent from the death) and contrary to the deliberate tactical way the defence was run at trial. Where an accused gives evidence that excludes a scenario, that scenario cannot found a reasonable doubt; disbelieving an accused does not let an appeal court treat his evidence as never given and substitute its own conjecture. The Court reaffirmed that in a circumstantial case guilt must be the only rational inference on the whole of the evidence, which must be considered as a whole and not piecemeal. It held there is no rule that post-offence concealment and lies are always "intractably neutral" as between murder and manslaughter — the lengths of concealment may support an inference of intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm. The Court of Appeal erred in dismissing the affair, financial stress and looming exposure as not capable of being relevant to motive and intent. Setting aside a jury verdict as unreasonable is a serious step given the jury's central constitutional role.
whatItMeans: This is a leading authority on building and proving circumstantial murder cases and on the evidentiary value of consciousness-of-guilt evidence. For your casework it confirms that lies, concealment of the body, and post-offence conduct are not automatically neutral on intent — the scale and nature of concealment can support an inference of murderous intent, so gather and document that conduct thoroughly. Motive evidence (relationship strain, an affair, financial pressure, looming exposure) is relevant to proving intent even where there is no premeditation, so build out the surrounding relationship and circumstantial picture. Remember the prosecution must exclude all reasonable hypotheses consistent with innocence, but a "bare possibility" or speculation is not a reasonable hypothesis — and where facts lie solely within a suspect's knowledge, unsupported innocent explanations carry little weight (Weissensteiner). Present the evidence as a whole, not piecemeal.
verdict: AWARENESS authority: it does not change investigative procedure, but it shapes how you assemble circumstantial proof of intent and treat post-offence conduct and lies as probative rather than neutral.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of New South Wales
outcome: Appeal against conviction dismissed; convictions for the LOGOZZO offences upheld. The admissions made on 15/05/2002 were properly admitted.
weight: High — unanimous result of the High Court of Australia (4:1 on reasoning, Kirby J dissenting), the apex court.
oneLine: High Court holds covertly recorded admissions to police were not unfair to use under s 90 of the Evidence Act 1995 (NSW), despite a deliberately incomplete caution and the suspect's mistaken belief his words could not be used.
whatHappened: EM was convicted of the murder of Joseph LOGOZZO and related offences arising from a 2002 home invasion in Sydney. Police already had unrecorded admissions from him about a separate home invasion (the KRESS offences, to which he pleaded guilty). On 24/04/2002 EM had refused to be recorded and was released. Police then obtained warrants under the Listening Devices Act 1984 (NSW) and, on 15/05/2002, took EM to a suburban park wearing covert recording devices. They told him he was not under arrest, was free not to talk, and repeatedly assured him they were not tricking him — but deliberately omitted the second part of the caution (that anything he said could be recorded and used in evidence). EM, who believed unrecorded conversations could not be used against him, made admissions about the LOGOZZO killing. The trial judge admitted the first part of the park conversation and excluded the part after a key statement by the detective. The Court of Criminal Appeal upheld that course.
whatHeld: The High Court (Gleeson CJ and Heydon J; Gummow and Hayne JJ agreeing; Kirby J dissenting) dismissed the appeal. It was not unfair under s 90 to use the admissions. Gummow and Hayne JJ reasoned that s 90 operates as a final "safety net" engaged only after the more specific provisions of the Act (ss 84, 85, 137, 138, 139), so that reliability (s 85) and impropriety in obtaining the evidence (s 138) fall to be dealt with under those sections, not s 90. The lead judgment of Gleeson CJ and Heydon J did not adopt that quarantine — it treated reliability as a factor relevant to fairness under s 90, and accepted s 90 can succeed even where no other ground does. EM had in any event failed to invoke or abandoned every other ground. Covert recording under a valid warrant is not made unfair merely because the suspect did not know he was being recorded, and EM conceded as much. His belief that what he said could not be used was inextricably linked to his belief it was not being recorded, so the same concession defeated the broader argument. The detectives merely failed to correct, rather than created, his mistaken belief. There was no legal obligation to caution him in these circumstances (s 139 conditions were not met, as he was not under arrest). The trial judge was also not required to give a special unreliability warning to the jury. Kirby J dissented, holding the deliberately half-given caution, the trickery and the choice of park venue derogated from the right to silence and rendered use of the evidence unfair.
whatItMeans: This case turns on the NSW uniform Evidence Act, which does not apply in WA — so treat it as guidance on principle, not as a rule you apply directly. The underlying message is about the common law unfairness discretion (the Lee/Swaffield line), which does operate in WA. The take-home for you: lawfully authorised covert recording of a suspect, even where the suspect wrongly assumes the chat is "off the record", will not generally be excluded as unfair — secret recording only works because the subject does not know. But note the warnings in this judgment. The majority required strict separation of issues: reliability, impropriety and proper cautioning are dealt with under specific rules, and a court can still exclude on those grounds. Deliberately giving only half a caution drew a strong dissent and is risky. Get and rely on proper authority for any listening device, keep the questioning fair (not hostile, overbearing or unduly persistent), and do not assume a covert operation cures a defective or absent caution where one is required. The safest practice remains a full and proper caution at the point one is legally required.
verdict: AWARENESS — it concerns the NSW Evidence Act unfairness discretion, which does not govern WA, but it usefully shows how appellate courts treat covertly recorded admissions and incomplete cautions.

appealFrom: Court of Appeal of the Supreme Court of Victoria
outcome: All four appeals dismissed; convictions stand. CLARKE granted leave to amend his notice of appeal but the new discretionary ground rejected.
weight: High Court of Australia, seven-judge bench, 6:1 majority (Kirby J dissenting). Binding and leading authority on the common law of confessions.
oneLine: The High Court (6:1) held that confessions obtained by the undercover "Mr Big"/scenario technique are admissible at common law: gang-impersonating police are not "persons in authority" and the confessions were voluntary.
whatHappened: Four men (TOFILAU, MARKS, HILL and CLARKE) were each suspected of murder in Victoria but there was insufficient evidence to charge. Victoria Police ran undercover "scenario" operations (the Canadian "Mr Big" model): officers posed as a criminal gang, drew each suspect into staged (fake) criminal activity, and offered gang membership, money and protection from prosecution through supposedly corrupt police — on condition the suspect told the gang "boss" the truth about the killing. Each man confessed to an undercover "boss". The confessions were admitted at trial and each was convicted. They appealed, arguing the confessions were involuntary because induced by persons in authority and/or lacked "basal voluntariness". CLARKE also argued discretionary exclusion.
whatHeld: Appeals dismissed (Kirby J dissenting). The undercover officers were not "persons in authority". A person is only a person in authority if the suspect perceives, on reasonable grounds, that they hold the lawful authority of the state to investigate or prosecute. The appellants believed they were dealing with criminals who could corruptly influence police, not with anyone wielding the lawful authority of the state — so the inducement rule was not engaged. The confessions were voluntary ("basal voluntariness" satisfied). There was no duress, intimidation, persistent importunity or sustained/undue pressure of a kind that overbore the will; the men spoke because they hoped to profit and avoid prosecution. Deception or trickery alone does not make a confession involuntary. On the discretion (CLARKE only), the trial judge made no error in admitting the evidence; the seriousness of the crime and the absence of any unlawful conduct by police weighed in favour of admission.
whatItMeans: This is the leading High Court authority validating the "Mr Big"/scenario covert technique for eliciting confessions in serious unsolved crimes. For your casework it means: covert confessions to officers posing as criminals can be admissible, because a suspect who thinks he is confessing to gangsters does not believe he is speaking to a person in authority and is not under state coercion. Two practical levers: avoid feeding the suspect facts only the offender would know (this protects reliability), and ensure the confession is recorded and independently corroborated (e.g. the suspect revealing where the murder weapon/scarf was). Be alert to the discretionary exclusion risk: courts will scrutinise proportionality (these techniques are justified by very serious crime), reliability, and forensic prejudice from showing the jury the suspect's willingness to join a criminal gang. Note this was decided on Victorian common law — confirm the current WA position and any controlled-operations/authorisation requirements before deploying such an operation.
verdict: ACTION — it confirms that scenario/"Mr Big" covert confessions are admissible and tells you exactly why (no person in authority, no overborne will), shaping how you design and document such operations.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of Tasmania (Marlow and Kelly (2001) 129 A Crim R 51)
outcome: Appeal dismissed; murder conviction upheld. Majority found the statement admissible; the minority would have excluded it but applied the proviso.
weight: High Court of Australia decision — authoritative on the construction principles (purposive construction, use of definitions) and on the continuing relevance of the McKinney warning, but the specific holding is confined to s 8 of a now-repealed Tasmanian Act.
tags: proviso / no substantial miscarriage of justice
oneLine: High Court holds a suspect's unrecorded admission made after a video interview ended was not "made in the course of official questioning" under Tasmanian law and so was admissible; conviction upheld.
whatHappened: Shane Leslie KELLY was convicted of murdering Tony George TANNER (killed on or about 23/11/1990) by a jury in the Supreme Court of Tasmania. During a video-recorded interview on 04/03/2000 KELLY denied involvement. After the interview was formally concluded at 9.17pm, and 30 to 60 minutes later, as he was about to be driven to hospital for blood and hair samples, KELLY said to police: "Sorry about the interview – no hard feelings, I was just playing the game... I suppose you will make notes of that as well." It was not in response to any question, was not recorded, and police did not take him back to record it. The defence argued this "impugned statement" was a confession or admission inadmissible under s 8(2)(a) of the Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas) because it was not video-taped. The trial judge admitted it; the Court of Criminal Appeal of Tasmania dismissed the appeal.
whatHeld: The majority (Gleeson CJ, Hayne and Heydon JJ) held that "in the course of official questioning" is a temporal expression marking the period from when questioning began to when it ceased. Questioning ended at 9.17pm when the interview was concluded; no further question triggered the statement, so it was not made in the course of official questioning and was admissible. The Court rejected the defence's attempts to read in "custody" or "within a reasonable time after" or "arising out of" — those ideas have no foothold in the statutory language. McHugh J and Kirby J dissented on construction, holding the statement should have been excluded, but agreed the appeal failed because the case against KELLY was overwhelming and the proviso (s 402(2) Criminal Code (Tas)) applied — no substantial miscarriage of justice. In surveying the law, the Court restated the long-standing concern about fabricated and unreliable confessions ("police verbals"); the continuing operation of the McKinney warning to juries about uncorroborated police-custody confessions was affirmed by Kirby J in dissent.
whatItMeans: This case is about how courts read recording-requirement statutes and the perennial danger of unrecorded "verbals". The practical lesson is to capture spontaneous admissions on video promptly — here police could easily have taken KELLY back to the interview room to put the statement to him on tape, and their failure to do so was criticised by every judge. Note carefully that this turns on a Tasmanian provision that has since been repealed, and WA's regime is different: under Criminal Code (WA) s 570D the admissibility of a confession does not turn on whether it was a response to questioning, so the "temporal" reasoning here does not transfer directly to your casework. Treat any post-interview, off-camera admission as fragile — record it or re-engage on camera, and expect the McKinney warning to apply to anything that is not reliably corroborated.
verdict: AWARENESS, not ACTION for WA: the holding construes a repealed Tasmanian section and WA's recording law operates on a different test, so use it for the principles and the recording lesson, not as authority on your charging or admissibility decisions.

court: Supreme Court of New South Wales
appealFrom: Local Court (HEILPERN LCM)
outcome: Magistrate's dismissals of the assault, resist and intimidate police charges quashed; matters remitted to the magistrate to be dealt with according to law, principally because the prosecution was denied procedural fairness over material the magistrate researched himself.
weight: Single-judge decision of the Supreme Court of NSW (SMART AJ) on a s.109(a) Justices Act 1902 review; reported at 127 A Crim R 151. Persuasive and frequently cited on the impropriety of arrest for minor offences, but a NSW first-instance authority applying NSW legislation — note WA has its own statutory framework.
oneLine: Using arrest for a minor offence when a summons or court attendance notice would suffice can be an "impropriety" under s.138 Evidence Act 1995 (NSW), exposing evidence of resulting resist/assault/intimidate charges to exclusion.
whatHappened: On 25/02/1999 at Wellington, Constable ROBINS attended a disturbance involving thrown rocks. Mr CARR, moderately intoxicated, swore at ROBINS and walked away still yelling. ROBINS arrested CARR for offensive language (maximum penalty a $660 fine). CARR resisted, a melee followed, and his shirt was torn; at the police station CARR allegedly threatened Constable SMITH and SMITH's family. CARR was charged with resisting, assaulting and intimidating police. ROBINS knew CARR by name and address, had a field court attendance notice (FCAN) book in the car, and conceded he arrested rather than summonsed because it was "far quicker". The magistrate (HEILPERN LCM) held the arrest, though lawful under s.352 Crimes Act 1900, was improper, and excluded the evidence under s.138 Evidence Act 1995, dismissing the three charges. The DPP sought review under s.109(a) Justices Act 1902.
whatHeld: SMART AJ held the magistrate was entitled to find the arrest improper within s.138. Lawfulness of an arrest does not make it proper. For minor offences where the offender's name and address are known, there is no risk of flight, and no reason to think a summons would be ineffective, arrest should not be used and a summons or CAN should be issued. "Improper" is not confined to bad faith, abuse of power or intentional wrongdoing; the test is objective and Ridgeway (entrapment, serious drug offence) sets the high threshold only for serious matters. The Court accepted that evidence of further offences precipitated by an ill-advised arrest can be "obtained in consequence of an impropriety" where the offences are closely connected and at the lower end of the scale (though not where they are moderately serious to serious). The magistrate's recklessness and seriousness findings were open. However, the magistrate denied the prosecution procedural fairness by relying on a Law Society Journal article, statistics and his own court experience without alerting the prosecutor (s.144(4) Evidence Act 1995). The dismissals were quashed and the matters remitted.
whatItMeans: Treat arrest for a minor offence as a last resort. Where you know a suspect's name and address, there is no flight risk, and a summons or court attendance notice would get them to court, you should proceed by summons/CAN, not arrest — even if the arrest is technically lawful under s.352 Crimes Act 1900. If you arrest unnecessarily and the suspect then resists, assaults or threatens police, the evidence of those new offences can be excluded under s.138 because it flowed from the improper arrest. The trifecta pattern (offensive language → arrest → resist/assault) is exactly what the court is warning against. Document your reasons for choosing arrest over summons; "it was quicker" or expedience will not survive scrutiny and can be found reckless. Note the limit: where the precipitated offence is moderately serious to serious (e.g. attempted murder, GBH), that evidence will still be admitted. Consider intoxication and whether threats were idle bluster when assessing intimidate-police charges.
verdict: AWARENESS-shifting authority on charging practice — it changes how you decide between arrest and summons for minor offences, so treat the practical guidance as ACTION-relevant even though the case itself is a procedural-fairness remittal.

appealFrom: Court of Appeal of the Supreme Court of Queensland
outcome: Appeal dismissed; convictions upheld. Although the trial judge erred in his identification directions (and, per the majority reasoning, in admitting the weapons evidence), the proviso applied because conviction was inevitable on the strength of the other evidence.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-judge bench — binding and the leading authority on the admissibility of, and warnings for, weak and irregularly obtained identification evidence. Applies and explains Domican v The Queen and Alexander v The Queen.
oneLine: FESTA confirms weak or irregularly obtained identification evidence is usually admissible (weight is for the jury), but sloppy identification procedures invite exclusion and demand specific jury warnings.
whatHappened: FESTA was jointly tried with co-accused RENTON for armed robberies of two National Australia Bank branches (Biggera Waters on 27/05/1996 and Paradise Point on 13/06/1996) and related unlawful use of vehicles. A man and woman committed the robberies, both disguised; the female wore a wig. The central issue was whether FESTA was that woman. Three witnesses (FYFFE, OGILVIE, JAMES) saw a woman acting suspiciously near the Biggera Waters scene; a fourth (HILL) saw a woman in a getaway car at Paradise Point. None could pick FESTA from photoboards. Police later asked the three Biggera Waters witnesses, who sat together at the Southport courthouse for the committal, to 'keep an eye out' for the woman — FESTA was effectively the only female of matching age and description present, and she was identified there. JAMES also relied on voice (six words) and gait. HILL picked FESTA's photo as merely 'similar'. Police did not hold an identification parade, kept no record, and let witnesses remain together. Weapons and ammunition of the same type as those used in the robberies, plus disguise items bearing FESTA's fingerprints, were found in RENTON's unit — but the guns had been bought on 17/06/1996, after the robberies. FESTA absconded mid-trial and was convicted in her absence.
whatHeld: The High Court dismissed the appeal. On admissibility, weakness of identification evidence is not a ground for exclusion; weak but relevant evidence is admissible unless its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice, and it then goes to weight for the jury (Gleeson CJ at [14], [23]; McHugh J at [51]). The trial judge's discretion to admit was open to him. Evidence is only unfairly prejudicial where the jury may misuse it or give it more weight than it deserves — not merely because it inculpates the accused ([22], [51]). Kirby J would have excluded the courthouse identification as 'virtually valueless' but prejudicial. The Court distinguished positive-identification evidence (which requires full Domican warnings) from circumstantial 'similarity' evidence. On the directions, a majority held the trial judge's directions on the courthouse identification were inadequate (Callinan J dissenting) for not sufficiently drawing out its specific weaknesses, and a majority (Gleeson CJ, Kirby and Hayne JJ) found the voice-identification direction inadequate too (McHugh and Callinan JJ holding it adequate). On the weapons and ammunition, a 3:2 majority (Gleeson CJ, Kirby and Callinan JJ) held they were properly admitted as relevant circumstantial evidence — the 'tools of trade' of bank robbers, not mere propensity; McHugh and Hayne JJ would have excluded them. Despite these errors, the case was so overwhelming that a properly directed jury would inevitably have convicted, so no substantial miscarriage of justice occurred under the proviso (Criminal Code (Q) s668E(1A)).
whatItMeans: This case is a direct warning about how you run identifications. Hold a proper identification parade where you can, and if you cannot, record why and use a large group of similar-looking people — never let a witness pick a suspect as that suspect walks into court, and never let identifying witnesses sit together or discuss what they saw. The Court repeatedly condemned police for ignoring the Queensland Operational Procedures Manual, which mirrors sound WA practice. Document the whole process — record, film or photograph it; the absence of any record was treated as a serious failing. Be alert to the 'displacement effect', the 'rogues' gallery effect' (photoboards suggesting a criminal history), and voice identifications based on only a few words. The reassuring point: even weak or irregularly obtained identification evidence will usually still be admitted, with the defects going to weight — but your sloppiness can still cost the conviction where the rest of the case is not strong. FESTA was saved only by overwhelming circumstantial evidence; do not assume the proviso will rescue a weak case. On weapons, note the result cuts the other way from a simple propensity bar: even items not used in the charged offence (here guns bought after the robberies) can be admissible as relevant circumstantial evidence — the 'tools of trade' of the kind of crime charged — where they are of the same character as those used and connect to the offending. Here a 3:2 majority held them properly admitted (McHugh and Hayne JJ dissenting).
verdict: AWARENESS-leaning but practically ACTION: it directly shapes how you conduct and record identifications and what 'tools of trade' evidence you can rely on, even though weak identification evidence remains admissible.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of New South Wales
outcome: AZZOPARDI: appeal allowed (5-2), conviction set aside, new trial ordered. DAVIS: special leave to appeal refused — the conviction stood, the case being overwhelming.
weight: High Court of Australia, full bench of seven Justices; authoritative, though majority reasoning was contested (McHugh J dissenting) and is anchored in the NSW statute.
oneLine: High Court holds a trial judge may comment on an accused's failure to give evidence only in rare cases where additional facts are peculiarly within the accused's knowledge; mere failure to contradict the prosecution case will never justify such comment.
whatHappened: Two NSW District Court trials were considered together. In AZZOPARDI, the accused was convicted of soliciting Daniel PAPALIA to murder Paul GAUCI; the Crown relied on direct evidence from PAPALIA and others, and AZZOPARDI did not give evidence. In DAVIS, the accused was convicted of three sexual offences against a nine-year-old girl; the Crown relied on the complainant's direct evidence supported by her mother and a doctor, and DAVIS did not give evidence. In both trials the judges told the jury they could take the accused's failure to give evidence into account when assessing the weight of the prosecution evidence. Both appealed, arguing the directions breached s20(2) Evidence Act 1995 (NSW) and earlier authority (RPS v The Queen).
whatHeld: The Court reconciled Weissensteiner and RPS. A judge may comment on an accused's failure to give evidence only in rare and exceptional cases — where the evidence is capable of explanation by additional facts known only to the accused. A comment is never warranted merely because the accused failed to contradict some part of the prosecution case, because mere contradiction is not evidence of any additional fact and an accused cannot be expected to give evidence. Any permissible comment is a comment (which the jury may disregard), not a direction, and must not, even by subtle allusion, suggest the accused stayed silent because he was, or believed he was, guilty (the prohibition in s20(2) is given full, broad operation). In AZZOPARDI the directions misstated Weissensteiner, were contradictory and breached s20(2); the proviso could not be applied. In DAVIS the directions were also wrong, but the case was overwhelming and special leave was refused (the conviction stood). The seven-judge Court split on Azzopardi: the plurality (Gaudron, Gummow, Kirby and Hayne JJ), with Callinan J, allowed the appeal; Gleeson CJ and McHugh J dissented (favouring the broad Weissensteiner reading and holding the comments lawful).
whatItMeans: This is a trial-direction case rather than an investigation rule, but it reinforces something useful for your casework: an accused's silence — whether at interview or at trial — cannot fill gaps in your brief. The prosecution case must stand on its own feet. Where guilt depends on facts that only the accused could explain, silence may carry weight; but where you have direct evidence (such as a complainant's account), the absence of an answer from the accused adds nothing. Build your brief so it proves the elements without relying on the accused having to respond. Note this turns on the NSW Evidence Act; WA practice is governed by the Evidence Act 1906 (WA), so confirm the local position before relying on the detail.
verdict: AWARENESS — it shapes how trial judges treat silence and confirms silence cannot prop up a weak brief, but it does not change WA investigative or charging procedure.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of the Supreme Court of South Australia
outcome: Appeal allowed; orders of the Court of Criminal Appeal of South Australia set aside; convictions on counts 2 to 7 quashed; retrial ordered. KIRBY J dissenting (would have dismissed the appeal).
weight: High Court of Australia, five-justice bench, majority of four (GLEESON CJ, GAUDRON, GUMMOW and CALLINAN JJ) with KIRBY J dissenting. Binding statement of principle on lies directions; refines the application of Edwards v The Queen (1993) 178 CLR 193.
oneLine: The High Court clarified when a jury must be given an Edwards-type "consciousness of guilt" direction about lies, holding such a direction was wrongly given in a false pretences trial where the prosecutor never relied on lies as proof of guilt.
whatHappened: ZONEFF was tried in the District Court of South Australia on seven counts of false pretences and fraudulent conversion arising from his work as a furniture salesman at the Le Cornu Furniture Centre. The prosecution alleged he ingratiated himself with five customers (Ms SNEATH, Mr McKINNON, Mr and Mrs DIK, and Ms PHILLIPS), persuaded them to pay money to him directly on false promises of discounts, bonus points and a joint building venture, and then used the money to pay his own personal debts. The total involved was about $73,000. He represented himself, gave evidence, and denied any fraudulent intent. In cross-examination the prosecutor put to him that various answers and prior statements were lies, and he admitted some were. Because he was unrepresented, the prosecutor (per South Australian practice) did not address the jury. The jury acquitted on count 1 (an alternative) and convicted on counts 2 to 7. The trial judge, on his own initiative, gave a direction referring to lies told out of a "consciousness of guilt". The Court of Criminal Appeal dismissed the conviction appeal by majority (OLSSON J dissenting).
whatHeld: The High Court allowed the appeal, quashed the convictions on counts 2 to 7 and ordered a retrial. An Edwards-type "consciousness of guilt" direction should, as a general rule, only be given if the prosecution contends a lie is evidence of guilt (told because the accused knew the truth would implicate him) and the lie is in fact capable of bearing that character. Here the prosecutor never put, expressly or by implication, that any answer was a lie told out of consciousness of guilt, so the direction was unnecessary and undesirable; it risked inviting the jury to infer guilt from lies on a basis the Crown never advanced. If there is a risk of confusion about how the prosecution puts its case, the trial judge should ask the prosecutor whether lies are relied on as consciousness of guilt and, if so, require the lie and its basis to be identified. Where lies go only to credit, a simple general warning suffices: the jury should not reason that because a person told a lie that is itself evidence of guilt, as people lie for many reasons. The majority was not satisfied the misdirection caused no substantial miscarriage in a false pretences case where dishonesty was readily misunderstood. KIRBY J dissented: he regarded the direction as a proper general (Broadhurst/Richens) warning that required nothing more — any imperfection was "infelicity, not error" — and held that, even if it were error, the proviso would apply given the overwhelming Crown case; he would have dismissed the appeal.
whatItMeans: This case is about jury directions, not investigative method, so it changes little in how you gather evidence. The practical lesson for your casework is conceptual: distinguish between lies that merely damage an accused's credibility and lies that positively indicate guilt (the "consciousness of guilt" category). A lie only shows consciousness of guilt where the accused told it knowing the truth would implicate him in the offence — a demanding test that many lies will not meet. When you build a brief and flag an accused's lies or false statements, be precise about which category you think a lie falls into and why, because the prosecutor will need to decide whether to run it as proof of guilt or merely as credibility. Do not assume that proving an accused lied equals proof of guilt — there may be innocent reasons (panic, protecting someone, concealing other embarrassing conduct). Identify the specific lie, the surrounding circumstances, and any independent evidence showing the truth would implicate the accused, so the lie can properly be relied on.
verdict: AWARENESS only — this governs jury directions on lies, not investigative practice, but it sharpens how you should characterise and document an accused's lies in a brief.

appealFrom: Court of Appeal of the Supreme Court of Victoria (R v Osland [1998] 2 VR 636)
outcome: Appeal dismissed (3:2). Conviction for murder upheld. Gaudron and Gummow JJ would have allowed the appeal and ordered a new trial.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-justice bench, majority of four. Binding and authoritative on acting in concert, inconsistency of verdicts, lies directions and battered wife syndrome. Note the concert principles are the majority view (McHugh, Kirby, Callinan JJ); Gaudron and Gummow JJ dissented on inconsistency.
oneLine: HIGH COURT (3:2) dismisses OSLAND's murder appeal: a person acting in concert and present at the scene is liable as a principal for the perpetrator's acts even if the perpetrator is acquitted, and a jury's failure to convict the co-accused does not necessarily render the conviction inconsistent.
whatHappened: On 30/07/1991 Heather OSLAND and her son David ALBION killed Frank OSLAND, OSLAND's husband and ALBION's step-father. They dug a grave during the day, OSLAND mixed sedatives into the deceased's dinner, and after he fell asleep ALBION struck the fatal blows to the head with an iron pipe in OSLAND's presence. They buried the body and OSLAND engaged in extensive deceptions, reporting him as a missing person. Both raised self-defence and provocation against a background of years of alleged violent and tyrannical behaviour; expert evidence of "battered wife syndrome" was led. At the joint trial the jury convicted OSLAND of murder but could not agree on ALBION; at a later retrial ALBION was acquitted. OSLAND appealed to the High Court.
whatHeld: The Court (McHugh, Kirby, Callinan JJ; Gaudron and Gummow JJ dissenting) dismissed the appeal. Where two or more people reach an understanding to commit a crime and are present at the scene, each is liable as a principal in the first degree for the acts of the actual perpetrator, regardless of the part each played (acting in concert). The liability is primary, not derivative: the person acting in concert can be convicted even if the actual perpetrator is acquitted (e.g. for self-defence, provocation, duress or insanity), because it is the perpetrator's acts, not their crime, that are attributed. The Court held there was no inconsistency between OSLAND's conviction and the failure to convict ALBION — the jury was entitled to find OSLAND did not act in self-defence or under provocation while remaining unable to decide whether the Crown had negatived ALBION's defences. ALBION's later acquittal at a separate trial provided no basis to acquit OSLAND. On the lies (a point analysed chiefly by Gaudron and Gummow JJ, whose reasoning on this the majority did not disturb), the Court held that where there is a risk the jury might treat lies (or threatening intercepted conversations) as evidence of guilt, the trial judge should ascertain how the prosecution puts the evidence and, if relied on as consciousness of guilt, direct the jury per Edwards v The Queen; if not, direct that it goes only to credit. The Court accepted that battered wife syndrome is a proper subject of expert evidence where the accused's responses lie outside ordinary experience, but it must be related to the issues (self-defence, provocation) and is not a separate defence. Gaudron and Gummow JJ dissented, finding the conviction inconsistent because a perpetrator cannot act under provocation/self-defence and simultaneously pursuant to a prior agreement to kill.
whatItMeans: What it means for your casework: when you investigate and charge a joint killing, remember that a participant present at the scene who agreed to the crime can be charged and convicted as a principal even if the actual perpetrator is not convicted — build your brief to prove the agreement/understanding, the accused's presence, and their intent independently. The understanding need not be express and may be inferred from all the circumstances. Note WA's equivalent provisions are Criminal Code (WA) ss 7-9 (referenced in the judgment), so the concert principle has direct local application. On evidence of lies told by a suspect (false missing-person reports, cover stories) and threats made in intercepted calls, anticipate that these will need careful judicial direction: identify precisely how the evidence proves guilt versus merely affecting credit, because there may be innocent explanations (e.g. family loyalty, panic). Gather and preserve material that shows the lie reveals knowledge of the offence. Where an accused will run battered wife/abusive-relationship evidence, expect expert evidence to be admissible and relevant to self-defence and provocation — collect a thorough history of the relationship, prior reports (or lack of them), and any evidence (like prior solicitation to kill, financial motive, premeditation such as a pre-dug grave, or intercepts showing violence had ceased) that bears on whether the response was genuinely defensive or premeditated.
verdict: AWARENESS-leaning but practically ACTION: this changes how you frame charges and assemble evidence in joint-enterprise homicides and how you handle lies and consciousness-of-guilt material, so treat it as affecting your casework.

appealFrom: Court of Appeal of Queensland (Swaffield) and Court of Appeal of Victoria (Pavic)
outcome: Both appeals dismissed. SWAFFIELD's acquittal of arson (entered by the Queensland Court of Appeal) stood; PAVIC's murder conviction stood. Kirby J dissented in PAVIC, favouring a new trial.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-Justice bench; a leading authority on the admissibility of confessions and the unfairness and public policy discretions. Binding and frequently applied.
oneLine: Covert recordings of admissions by an undercover police officer or police agent may be excluded where the suspect, having already exercised the right to silence, has admissions actively elicited from him in derogation of his free choice to speak or stay silent.
whatHappened: Two joined appeals. SWAFFIELD was charged with arson, breaking and entering, and stealing relating to a fire at the Leichhardt Rowing Club. He twice declined to answer police questions and the original charges were dropped when no evidence was offered at committal. Months later, during an undercover drug operation, Constable MARSHALL — posing as a purchaser of illegal drugs and not cautioning SWAFFIELD — covertly recorded conversations in which SWAFFIELD admitted involvement in the arson. He was re-charged and convicted. PAVIC was suspected of the murder of Andrew ASTBURY, whose handcuffed body was found in the Yarra River. On 03/01/1995 PAVIC, on his solicitor's advice, made no comment to police and was released. Police then fitted his close friend CLANCY with a recording device. CLANCY, who feared he himself was suspected, engaged PAVIC in conversation and elicited inculpatory admissions. PAVIC was convicted of murder. Neither confession was said to be involuntary or illegally obtained.
whatHeld: A majority re-expressed the approach to confessional evidence: admissibility turns first on voluntariness, then on reliability, then on a discretion to exclude covering unfairness to the accused and public policy. Toohey, Gaudron and Gummow JJ held the common law should be developed around the suspect's right to choose whether or not to speak; where that freedom is impugned, the court has a discretion to exclude. Adopting the Canadian approach (Hebert, Broyles), the focus is whether admissions were actively elicited by police or a police agent, rather than merely overheard. In SWAFFIELD, the undercover officer actively elicited the arson admissions in clear breach of his right to choose whether to speak; the Crown's appeal was dismissed, so his conviction remained quashed (his acquittal stood). In PAVIC, the majority held nothing improper occurred: CLANCY was trusted by PAVIC, the admissions indicated reliability, and there was no sufficient reason to interfere — appeal dismissed. Kirby J dissented on PAVIC, holding the police exploited the relationship of trust between the two friends to circumvent PAVIC's exercised right to silence, and would have ordered a new trial.
whatItMeans: When you use undercover officers or co-operating civilians wired to record a suspect, the key legal risk is active elicitation. Merely listening to or recording spontaneous admissions is generally acceptable; questioning that is the functional equivalent of an interrogation is not, especially where the suspect has already exercised the right to silence. The distinction matters most where the operative is, or is acting as an agent of, a police officer. Be aware that targeting a suspect who has declined to answer questions — and using a disguise or a trusted friend to get what they refused to tell police — invites exclusion under the unfairness and public policy discretions. It is not unlawful to use deception, ruses or recording technology in investigations, and reliability of the admission and seriousness of the offence weigh in favour of admission. But exploiting a relationship of trust to extract a confession the suspect would not otherwise make is the line to watch. Document why questioning was conversational rather than an interrogation, and be cautious where the target has already refused to speak.
verdict: AWARENESS — this is a foundational High Court statement of principle on confession admissibility that shapes how covert recording operations are assessed, but it sets discretionary standards rather than fixed investigative rules.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of the Supreme Court of Victoria
outcome: Appeal allowed; convictions quashed; order of the Victorian Court of Criminal Appeal set aside; new trial ordered (the High Court noting the decision whether to proceed was for the Crown given time already served).
weight: High Court of Australia, five-judge bench, unanimous. Binding statement of principle on the relationship between statutory jury-warning provisions and the common law Kilby direction, and on appellate review of a refusal to discharge a jury.
oneLine: High Court quashed sexual-offence convictions and ordered a retrial because the trial judge wrongly directed the jury that delay in complaint could not be used at all, and should also have discharged the jury after apparently deliberate inadmissible propensity evidence.
whatHappened: Ian CROFTS was convicted in the County Court of Victoria of one count of an indecent act with a child under 16 and four counts of sexual penetration of a child between 10 and 16, all involving the same complainant on a single night between September 1991 and May 1992. He was acquitted on the other counts. The complainant first complained in June 1993 — about six years after the earliest alleged incident and six months after the last. Two problems arose at trial. First, despite a ruling excluding evidence of other uncharged sexual misconduct, the prosecutor in re-examination led the complainant into describing acts that happened "many times", and the judge refused to discharge the jury, instead giving a general warning. Second, the judge directed the jury that, as a matter of law, they could not draw any inference from the delay in complaint, then gave the statutory s 61(1)(b) warning that delay does not necessarily mean the allegation is false — without telling the jury they were still entitled to take the delay into account when assessing the complainant's credibility. The Victorian Court of Criminal Appeal dismissed the appeal; CROFTS appealed to the High Court.
whatHeld: The appeal was allowed unanimously. On the delay direction: s 61 of the Crimes Act 1958 (Vic) did not abolish the principle in Kilby v The Queen that absence of, or delay in, complaint is relevant to a complainant's credibility (though not to consent and not as proof of the facts). The statute was meant to correct the balance, not remove it — it stops judges treating complainants as an unreliable class, but does not stop a judge telling the jury that substantial, unexplained delay may be taken into account in assessing credibility. The judge erred by telling the jury, as a matter of law, that they could not use the delay, and then failed to give the balancing Kilby direction. On the jury discharge: although discharge is discretionary and judged by necessity, the appellate court must itself decide whether the refusal risked a substantial miscarriage. The inadmissible evidence was highly prejudicial, came from apparently deliberate questioning at the close of the complainant's evidence before a long adjournment, and could not be cured by a general direction. The majority (Toohey, Gaudron, Gummow and Kirby JJ) held the discretion miscarried and the jury should have been discharged; Dawson J dissented on this ground, holding the discretion did not miscarry. For the majority, either error alone required the convictions to be quashed (Dawson J would have quashed on the delay-direction ground alone).
whatItMeans: This is mainly a trial-direction case, but it shapes how you should think about delayed complaints in sexual matters. Delay is not fatal and does not prove consent or fabrication — but it remains legitimately relevant to a complainant's credibility, and the longer and less explained the delay, the more weight it may carry at trial. When you take a statement involving a long gap before reporting, capture and record the reasons for the delay (fear, guilt, family pressure, age, confusion) — the prosecution can use those explanations, and your file should document them. Second, the case is a strong reminder about propensity/uncharged-conduct evidence: where a judge has ruled it inadmissible, eliciting it — even by a single broad question — can collapse the trial. Brief witnesses carefully before they testify so they keep to the charged acts, and make sure the prosecutor knows the boundaries of any exclusion ruling. Deliberate or careless questioning that opens up excluded material can wreck an otherwise sound case.
verdict: AWARENESS-level authority: it does not change how you gather evidence, but it confirms that delay in complaint remains relevant to credibility and warns that breaching an exclusion ruling on uncharged conduct can cause a mistrial.

appealFrom: Supreme Court of New South Wales (Court of Criminal Appeal) — R v McAuliffe (1993) 70 A Crim R 303
outcome: Both appeals dismissed; murder convictions affirmed.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-judge unanimous (per curiam) bench. Leading authority on extended common purpose / joint criminal enterprise, though later considered and modified by subsequent High Court authority.
oneLine: McAULIFFE confirms that a party to a joint criminal enterprise is liable for a further crime he personally foresaw as a possible incident and continued to participate with that foresight, even if it fell outside the agreed common purpose.
whatHappened: Late on 20/07/1990 the McAuliffe brothers (Sean, 17; David, 16) and Matthew DAVIS, after drinking and smoking marijuana, went to a clifftop park near Bondi Beach to rob or bash someone. Sean carried a hammer; Davis carried a stick. The brothers were experienced in Tae Kwon Do. They set upon two men. Sean robbed and assaulted SULLIVAN, while David and DAVIS attacked RATTANAJATURATHAPORN, punching, kicking and beating him with the stick. Sean then side-kicked the deceased in the chest "to finish the fight", knocking him from a footpath onto rocks 3-5m below, a short distance from the cliff edge. The men left. The deceased's body was later found in the sea at the bottom of the cliff; death was caused by injuries from the fall and drowning. DAVIS pleaded guilty to murder and did not give evidence. The brothers were convicted of murder and of separate offences against SULLIVAN. The Court of Criminal Appeal dismissed their appeals, and they appealed to the High Court by special leave.
whatHeld: Appeals dismissed. The trial judge correctly directed the jury. The Court held that a party to a joint criminal enterprise is guilty of any crime falling within the scope of the common purpose, the scope being determined subjectively by what the parties contemplated. Beyond that, a party is also guilty of a crime falling outside the scope of the common purpose if he contemplated as a possibility the commission of that offence by another party in carrying out the joint enterprise and continued to participate with that knowledge. The Court rejected the appellants' argument that the intentional infliction of grievous bodily harm had to be agreed (expressly or tacitly) by all parties before one party could be liable for murder. Individual foresight of the possible incident, coupled with continued participation, is sufficient — the criminal culpability lies in participating in the venture with that foresight. Where the prosecution relies on this extended limb it must prove the individual accused foresaw the incidental crime might be committed and cannot rely on the common purpose to establish that state of mind. The Court also upheld the causation directions, applying Royall v The Queen on escape/self-preservation cases.
whatItMeans: This is a foundational complicity case for any group-violence or joint-enterprise investigation. When you build a case against a secondary party, you have two routes: (1) the agreed common purpose, where everything within the understanding (express or tacit) makes all parties equally liable; and (2) the extended common purpose, where a party is liable for a further offence he personally foresaw as a possible incident and kept participating anyway. For the second route your evidence must go to the individual's actual state of mind — what he knew and foresaw — so gather material showing each suspect's knowledge (e.g. that another carried a weapon, comments made, prior conduct, awareness of others' fighting skills) and that he continued in the venture with that knowledge. You cannot simply infer foresight from the shared plan alone. Note this reflects the common law as it then stood; be aware the High Court later revisited extended joint enterprise in Miller v The Queen [2016], so check the current state of the law before relying on it.
verdict: AWARENESS — this is settled appellate doctrine on complicity that shapes how you frame and evidence joint-enterprise cases rather than a new investigative procedure, so treat it as essential background knowledge.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of South Australia
outcome: Appeal against conviction dismissed; murder conviction upheld.
weight: Very high — a unanimous High Court of Australia decision and the leading authority establishing the test for admissibility of propensity and similar fact evidence in Australia.
oneLine: The High Court upheld PFENNIG's murder conviction and set the test for admitting propensity/similar fact evidence: it is admissible only if there is no reasonable view of it consistent with the accused's innocence.
whatHappened: PFENNIG was convicted of the murder of ten-year-old Michael BLACK, who disappeared from Sturt Reserve, Murray Bridge, South Australia on or about 18/01/1989. The boy's body was never found. The case was entirely circumstantial: PFENNIG was present at the reserve in his Kombi van, admitted speaking to Michael and lending him a knife, and was, on the evidence, among the last seen with the boy; the boy's bike and belongings were later found neatly stacked at Thiele Reserve, suggesting a false trail to imply drowning. About a year later (30/12/1989) PFENNIG abducted and sexually assaulted a 13-year-old boy, H., luring him into the same van, binding and gagging him, and disposing of H.'s bike to suggest an accident. He pleaded guilty to those offences. He also told his former wife he had been "thinking of it on and off for the past twelve months". The trial judge admitted the H. evidence on a voir dire. The Court of Criminal Appeal of SA dismissed his appeal, and he appealed to the High Court arguing the H. evidence was wrongly admitted.
whatHeld: Appeal dismissed. The plurality (Mason CJ, Deane and Dawson JJ) held (the appeal was dismissed unanimously) that propensity or similar fact evidence is admissible only where its probative force is so high that, viewed in the context of the prosecution case, there is no reasonable view of it consistent with the innocence of the accused. Because such evidence is a special class of circumstantial evidence, the trial judge must apply the same test a jury applies to circumstantial evidence. "Striking similarity" or "underlying unity" is not an essential prerequisite, but the evidence will usually lack the required probative force without such features. Here, once drowning was excluded and abduction for a sexual purpose was the only rational explanation, the H. evidence (the same van, same modus operandi, the false trail, and his admissions) had sufficient cogency that its probative force outweighed its prejudicial effect. Toohey J and McHugh J agreed the evidence was admissible, McHugh J holding it went to proving the abduction and murder, not just identity.
whatItMeans: This case gives you the governing standard — the Pfennig test — for whether evidence of an accused's other criminal conduct (prior offences, similar incidents, propensity) can be used. When you are building a case that relies on uncharged conduct or prior offending to support an inference of guilt, ask whether that evidence is so cogent that there is no reasonable view of it consistent with innocence. Mere bad character or a general disposition to offend will never get in — it is highly prejudicial and lacks cogency. What lifts such evidence to admissibility is a distinctive pattern, modus operandi, or "signature" tying the incidents together, combined with opportunity and other circumstantial links. Document the unusual or distinctive features (here: the van, luring a young boy, the false trail) and the surrounding circumstantial evidence carefully. Be alert that the more prejudicial the material, the higher the cogency required before a court will admit it.
verdict: AWARENESS — this is the foundational High Court authority on propensity/similar fact evidence; it shapes how you assess and assemble such evidence rather than altering a specific investigative procedure.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of the Supreme Court of New South Wales
outcome: Appeal allowed. Order of the NSW Court of Criminal Appeal set aside; convictions quashed and verdicts of acquittal entered. Brennan J and McHugh J dissented; Gaudron J would have ordered a retrial on lesser charges.
weight: High Court of Australia, full bench of seven; the leading and binding statement of the unsafe/unsatisfactory test, applied across Australia.
oneLine: M v THE QUEEN sets the High Court test for when an appellate court will quash a jury conviction as unsafe or unsatisfactory.
whatHappened: M was convicted in the District Court in Sydney of two counts of indecently assaulting his 13-year-old daughter and three counts of sexual intercourse with her, the offences alleged to have occurred over two weekends in September 1990. The complainant's evidence was uncorroborated and M denied the allegations to police and on oath at trial. There were discrepancies in the complainant's account (what was on television, whether tennis was on, why she slept in a particular room), no early complaint to her mother or stepmother, apparently normal conduct at a barbecue the next day, and medical evidence that her hymen was intact. The Court of Criminal Appeal of NSW dismissed his appeal (Sully J expressing personal unease but feeling bound by authority). M appealed to the High Court on the single ground that the verdicts were unsafe and unsatisfactory.
whatHeld: The High Court (Mason CJ, Deane, Dawson and Toohey JJ in the joint judgment, Gaudron J agreeing on the test but differing on disposition) allowed the appeal and entered acquittals. The Court gave authoritative guidance on the unsafe/unsatisfactory test under common-form criminal appeal provisions (here Criminal Appeal Act 1912 (NSW) s 6(1)): the question is whether, upon the whole of the evidence, it was open to the jury to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the accused was guilty. The appellate court must make its own independent assessment but must give full weight to the jury's advantage in seeing and hearing the witnesses. Where the evidence, on the record, contains discrepancies, displays inadequacies, is tainted or otherwise lacks probative force such that, even allowing for the jury's advantage, there is a significant possibility that an innocent person has been convicted, the court is bound to set the verdict aside. On the facts, the lack of corroboration, the inconsistent medical evidence, the absence of complaint, and the inherent improbability of the conduct (a squeaky bed in an unlocked room in a small, fully occupied house) meant the jury should have had a reasonable doubt. Brennan J and McHugh J dissented, emphasising the jury's role; Gaudron J would have ordered a retrial on lesser charges, finding only the penetration element doubtful.
whatItMeans: What it means for your casework: this is the leading authority on appellate review of jury verdicts, so it sits in the background of every brief, but it carries a practical warning for how you build a case. A conviction can be quashed where uncorroborated complainant evidence is undercut by internal discrepancies, inconsistent medical evidence, and inherent improbability. When you investigate, work to shore up reliability: pin down peripheral details that can be checked (what was actually on television, room layouts, who was present), secure recent-complaint witnesses, and obtain clear, properly-framed medical evidence that addresses the specific element charged. Note the penetration point: the prosecution had to prove penetration of the vagina in its strict anatomical sense for the sexual intercourse charges, and the medical evidence did not establish it — so make sure the charge matches what the evidence actually proves, and consider lesser/alternative charges where penetration is uncertain. Be alert that honest discrepancies on detail are not fatal, but accumulated weaknesses in a wholly uncorroborated case can be.
verdict: AWARENESS — this fixes the appellate standard for reviewing verdicts rather than changing frontline procedure, but it directly informs how robustly you should corroborate and charge cases that turn on a single witness.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of Queensland
outcome: Appeal allowed (3-2); conviction quashed and an acquittal entered. The 'Edwards direction' comes from the joint reasons of Deane, Dawson and Gaudron JJ.
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: A lie shows consciousness of guilt only if it is deliberate, material, and told because the truth would implicate — otherwise it just goes to credit.
whatHappened: The High Court's statement of when an accused's lie can be used as evidence of guilt rather than merely as a matter of credit. On the actual facts the Court (3-2) allowed EDWARDS' appeal and entered an acquittal: his lie (denying he was a police informer) was not probative of guilt because the innocent explanation was so plausible, so it went only to credit.
whatHeld: Per Deane, Dawson and Gaudron JJ (the 3-2 majority; Brennan and McHugh JJ dissenting): a lie can be evidence of a consciousness of guilt only where it is (1) deliberate, (2) material — about a circumstance connected to the offence, and (3) told because the truth would implicate the accused. A lie failing those limbs goes only to credit, and the jury must be directed carefully so ordinary lies aren't treated as proof of guilt.
whatItMeans: When a suspect gives a false account it can become evidence, not just bad credit — but only if it meets the three limbs. So lock the account down in free recall before you challenge: pin them to a clear, detailed version first, so a later lie is demonstrably deliberate and material.
verdict: Changes interview craft: secure the account before the challenge so a lie can carry evidential weight.

appealFrom: Court of Appeal (Court of Criminal Appeal) of Queensland
outcome: Appeal dismissed (5-2). Convictions for the murders of BAYERL and ZACK and theft of the Immanuel upheld. GAUDRON and McHUGH JJ dissented, would have allowed the appeal and ordered a new trial.
weight: High Court of Australia, seven-judge bench, majority decision — binding and a leading authority on the evidentiary effect of an accused's failure to testify.
oneLine: WEISSENSTEINER v The Queen confirms a jury may more safely draw an inference of guilt where an accused stays silent on facts peculiarly within his knowledge that, if innocent, would call for explanation.
whatHappened: WEISSENSTEINER answered an advertisement to work on a 36-foot cutter, the Immanuel, owned by Hartwig BAYERL, who lived aboard with his pregnant fiancee Susan ZACK. The three prepared the boat for a Pacific cruise. BAYERL and ZACK were last seen aboard with WEISSENSTEINER near Fitzroy Island in late November 1989 and then vanished, ceasing all contact with their families and bank accounts. WEISSENSTEINER was thereafter seen alone in sole possession of the boat, which still held the couple's clothes, jewellery, opals, BAYERL's treasured bible, camping gear and concealed rifles. He sailed the Immanuel around the Pacific, repainted its name, and told a series of inconsistent stories about where the couple were and how he came to possess the boat. He was detained in the Marshall Islands in August 1990, interviewed, attempted to escape, and was returned to Cairns and charged on 06/09/1990. The prosecution case was entirely circumstantial. He gave no evidence and called no evidence at trial. He was convicted of two murders and theft of the vessel.
whatHeld: By majority (5-2), the High Court dismissed the appeal and upheld the trial judge's direction. The Court held that where the prosecution proves facts that call for explanation, and the explanation (if any) must be peculiarly within the accused's knowledge, the jury may more safely draw an available inference of guilt from the proven facts when the accused elects not to give evidence of those facts. This is distinct from drawing guilt merely from silence: silence is not evidence, not an admission, and cannot fill gaps or act as a 'make-weight' where the prosecution case is otherwise insufficient. The failure to testify only operates to discount innocent hypotheses where the evidence supporting them, if it exists, lies within the accused's knowledge. The majority distinguished Petty v The Queen (which concerns pre-trial silence to police) as not governing the position at trial. The Court noted that in Queensland (and other jurisdictions without a statutory prohibition) a judge may comment on the accused's failure to give evidence, but the jury must always be reminded that the onus stays on the prosecution and the accused need not testify. GAUDRON and McHUGH JJ dissented, holding the directions defective for being framed in terms of failure to give evidence rather than the specific unexplained facts.
whatItMeans: This case underpins how circumstantial cases work where the accused alone holds the key facts. Build your case so that proven facts squarely call for an explanation that only the suspect could give — here, sole possession of a missing couple's boat and personal effects, plus inconsistent accounts. The stronger and more pointed your circumstantial evidence, the more safely a jury may draw an inference already available on the evidence — the accused's silence adds no probative weight of its own, it only makes it safer to discount innocent explanations that would lie within his knowledge. Silence is never a substitute for proof: you must first assemble evidence sufficient on its own to warrant the inference before the accused's silence adds anything. Document inconsistent accounts, lies about whereabouts, and possession of items the victims would never voluntarily abandon — these are exactly the facts that demand explanation. Note the line from Petty: this principle applies to silence at trial, not to a suspect's exercise of the right to silence when questioned by police — do not treat refusal to answer your questions as evidence of guilt. The dissent's caution shows directions must be tied to specific unexplained facts, so make your evidence pinpoint exactly what the suspect, and only the suspect, can explain. Caveat: Weissensteiner was substantially narrowed by Azzopardi v The Queen (2001) 205 CLR 50 — such directions are now confined to exceptional cases where there are additional facts peculiarly within the accused's knowledge; treat this as the foundation of the rule, not its current outer limit.
verdict: AWARENESS — this is an evidentiary and trial-direction principle; it shapes how you build and frame circumstantial cases but does not change investigative powers or procedure.

appealFrom: New South Wales Court of Criminal Appeal
outcome: Appeal allowed; Court of Criminal Appeal order set aside; conviction quashed and verdict of acquittal entered; no new trial.
weight: High — unanimous High Court of Australia decision; leading authority on unlawful arrest for questioning and exclusion of confessions for unfairness and on public policy grounds.
oneLine: High Court holds a confession obtained while a suspect was unlawfully arrested and detained solely for questioning should have been excluded for unfairness and on public policy grounds; conviction quashed.
