whatHappened: Stephen FOSTER, a 21-year-old Aboriginal man, was charged with maliciously setting fire to the Narooma High School (fire on 04/08/1987). On 14/08/1987 eight police arrived at Wallaga Lake, ordered FOSTER into the back of a caged police truck and took him to Narooma Police Station. He denied involvement but within an hour signed a seven-line typed confession, which was the only evidence against him. The trial judge ran two voir dire hearings, found the arrest was not merely for questioning, ruled the arrest lawful, found the confession voluntary, and admitted it. FOSTER was convicted. The NSW Court of Criminal Appeal accepted the arrest and interrogation were unlawful but held the trial judge's discretion had not miscarried. The Crown conceded on appeal that the arrest was unlawful, being solely for interrogation, as police had neither the intention nor the evidence to charge him at the time of arrest.
whatHeld: The High Court allowed the appeal, quashed the conviction and entered an acquittal with no retrial (FOSTER had already served his sentence). The majority (Mason CJ, Deane, Dawson, Toohey, Gaudron JJ) held that arrest solely for the purpose of questioning is unlawful and that the trial judge's error about the lawfulness of the arrest meant his discretion miscarried. Exercising the discretion afresh, they held the confession should have been excluded as unfair to the accused: the unlawful arrest and detention were serious and reckless, FOSTER was denied a lawyer, isolated from any non-police witness, semi-illiterate and especially vulnerable, and his detention destroyed any chance of corroborating his account or his allegations of threats. They also held it should have been excluded on public policy grounds, as the case showed deliberate or reckless disregard of the law by those who enforce it. Brennan J agreed, focusing on the flawed voluntariness finding and unfairness. McHugh J agreed the voluntariness finding was flawed (because a confession can have multiple causes and the judge wrongly treated it as either/or) and would have quashed the conviction, but would not have exercised the discretions afresh.
whatItMeans: This is a binding High Court authority and a cornerstone for how you handle arrest and confessions. You cannot arrest a person merely to question them — arrest must be for the purpose of bringing the person before a court to be charged, and you need the intention and evidence to charge. Detaining a suspect for interrogation without a lawful basis can taint any confession and lead to its exclusion for unfairness or on public policy grounds, even if it appears voluntary. The judgment stresses the vulnerability of suspects held in isolation, especially young, Aboriginal or poorly educated people, and the value of independent corroboration — secure a recording (audio/video) and avoid isolating the suspect from any non-police witness. On voluntariness, remember a confession can have more than one cause: if a threat or pressure played any substantial part, the confession is not voluntary even if something else (like seeing a co-accused's statement) was the final trigger. Note this case applies NSW legislation; in your work apply the equivalent WA arrest and confession rules, but the common law principles on unlawful arrest and the exclusionary discretions are authoritative.
verdict: ACTION — it directly governs lawful arrest and how confessions must be obtained to survive challenge.

outcome: Mandatory cogent warning on disputed identification evidence
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: Where identification is significant and disputed, the judge must give a cogent, specific warning about its dangers — ID evidence is inherently risky.
whatHappened: The High Court on the directions required where disputed identification evidence is significant to the case. (Held at principle level here.)
whatHeld: Where a case depends on disputed identification evidence, the trial judge must give the jury a cogent, specific warning about the special dangers of ID evidence and the factors affecting its reliability. This was the majority's holding (Mason CJ, Deane, Dawson, Toohey, Gaudron and McHugh JJ; Brennan J dissenting on its application to the facts).
whatItMeans: Treat ID as fragile and process it properly — proper identification procedures, a contemporaneous note of the witness’s first description and confidence, no contamination. It will be scrutinised hard, so the integrity of how you obtained it is everything.
verdict: Drives the habit: rigorous, well-documented identification procedures.

caseName: Petty and Maiden v The Queen
appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of New South Wales
outcome: Appeals dismissed (5-2); convictions of PETTY and MAIDEN upheld. No adverse inference may be drawn from pre-trial silence — but the immunity did not assist Maiden, who had spoken.
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: No adverse inference may be drawn against a suspect for exercising the right to silence before trial.
whatHappened: The High Court confirmed the protection attaching to pre-trial silence. On the facts, though, the appeals were dismissed (5-2) and the convictions upheld: Maiden had not stayed silent — he gave a record of interview and an unsworn statement — so the silence immunity did not protect his later, inconsistent account.
whatHeld: A person's exercise of the right to silence before trial cannot be used to draw an adverse inference of guilt; the burden remains on the State. Critically, the immunity protects silence — it did not assist Maiden here, because he spoke (a record of interview and an unsworn statement), so the jury could weigh his late, inconsistent account. The Court split: Mason CJ, Deane, Toohey and McHugh JJ, with Dawson J, were against the appellants; Brennan and Gaudron JJ would have allowed the appeals.
whatItMeans: Don’t build your case on, or invite comment on, a suspect’s silence. A no-comment interview is their right — keep proving the case from the evidence, not from the silence. (The DSS’s ‘have a rip’ is about lawful persistence within the interview, not using silence against them.)
verdict: Drives the habit: never rely on or remark on a suspect’s silence.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of Tasmania (Nettlefold, Underwood and Wright JJ)
outcome: Appeal dismissed; murder conviction upheld. Trial judge correct to withhold provocation from the jury.
weight: High Court of Australia, unanimous seven-member bench — a leading and authoritative statement of principle on provocation.
oneLine: High Court sets out the objective test for provocation, holding personal characteristics may colour the gravity of the provocation but the standard of self-control stays that of an ordinary person (age aside).
whatHappened: In the early hours of 05/06/1988 at Scottsdale (Tas), STINGEL, then 19, stabbed Jason Scott TAYLOR in the chest with a butcher's knife, killing him. STINGEL had a long history of obsessive harassment of his former girlfriend ("A"), including threats to kill her, threats against her associates, and a breached restraining order. On the night, STINGEL found A and TAYLOR engaged in sexual activity in a parked car; TAYLOR told him "Piss off you cunt". STINGEL returned to his own car, retrieved the knife, and fatally stabbed TAYLOR. He was convicted of murder. The trial judge refused to leave provocation under s160 of the Criminal Code (Tas) to the jury; the Court of Criminal Appeal (Tas) upheld that. STINGEL appealed to the High Court.
whatHeld: The appeal was dismissed. In a unanimous joint judgment (Mason CJ, Brennan, Deane, Dawson, Toohey, Gaudron and McHugh JJ), the Court held that s160(2) imposes an objective threshold test: the wrongful act or insult must be capable of depriving an ordinary person of self-control to the degree, method and continuance of violence producing death. Personal characteristics of the accused (age, sex, race, history, relationships, even mental condition) may be used to identify the content and assess the gravity of the provocation, but the power of self-control measured is that of a hypothetical ordinary person, not the accused. The only attribute attributed to the ordinary person is age (where youthful immaturity is open). The Court also held "wrongful" qualifies "act" but not "insult", so an insult need not be wrongful; "insult" is given its ordinary, wide meaning and is not confined by old common-law limits. On the facts, no reasonable jury could doubt that an ordinary 19-year-old would not have reacted as STINGEL did — his infatuation and jealousy reduced his actual self-control but could not lower the objective standard.
whatItMeans: This is a foundational authority on provocation and the objective "ordinary person" test. For your casework it clarifies how to assess a claimed provocation defence: separate the gravity of the provocation (where the suspect's history, relationships and characteristics are relevant) from the standard of self-control (which stays objective and ordinary, only adjusted for age). When investigating a homicide where provocation may arise, gather full evidence of the relationship history, prior conduct, threats, and the deceased's words and actions at the time — these all bear on gravity. Note the threshold is high: the response must be capable of being provoked in an ordinary person to the degree of violence causing death. A suspect's obsessive jealousy or abnormal temperament does not lower the bar. Be aware the Crown bears the onus of disproving provocation beyond reasonable doubt once it is raised on the evidence.
verdict: AWARENESS — this is a leading statement of provocation principle that shapes how you understand and document a provocation issue, but it does not directly change WA investigative or charging procedure.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of New South Wales
outcome: Special leave to appeal granted; appeal dismissed.
weight: High Court of Australia, five Justices, unanimous in result. A leading and binding authority on the use of circumstantial evidence and the standard of proof for intermediate facts.
oneLine: Circumstantial cases: only indispensable intermediate facts (the "links in a chain") must be proved beyond reasonable doubt — not every item of evidence.
whatHappened: James William SHEPHERD was convicted of conspiring to import heroin into Australia. The Crown case rested on circumstantial evidence in three categories: evidence from two undercover officers who overheard Clark tell SHEPHERD to "take over the reins" of the drug organisation; evidence from accomplices granted immunity; and evidence of financial transactions said to show SHEPHERD and Clark shared the proceeds. The NSW Court of Criminal Appeal initially held the trial judge erred by not giving a so-called "Chamberlain direction" — that the jury could only infer guilt from facts proved beyond reasonable doubt. The Crown argued no such direction was required. The matter reached the High Court on the question of whether that direction must be given.
whatHeld: The High Court held that Chamberlain v The Queen [No 2] (1984) 153 CLR 521 is not authority for the proposition that a jury must be directed that they cannot use a fact to infer guilt unless that fact is proved beyond reasonable doubt. The criminal standard applies to the elements of the offence and to any intermediate fact that is an indispensable link in the chain of reasoning towards guilt — such a link must be proved beyond reasonable doubt. But where the evidence consists of "strands in a cable" rather than "links in a chain", no such direction is appropriate. Individual items of evidence used to build an inference do not each have to be proved beyond reasonable doubt; the probative force of evidence is cumulative. There is no invariable rule of law or practice that the direction be given in every circumstantial case — it is a matter for the trial judge depending on the case. SHEPHERD's case was a "cable" case: the jury only had to be satisfied, on the whole of the evidence, that no reasonable explanation consistent with innocence remained, which is what they were told. (McHugh J read Chamberlain as intending the broader proposition but agreed it is not authority requiring such a direction to a jury.)
whatItMeans: This shapes how you should think about building a circumstantial brief. Identify the indispensable links in your chain of proof — the intermediate facts that, if not established, cause the case to collapse (for example, that the accused was present, or that a substance was a particular drug). Those critical links must be capable of being proved beyond reasonable doubt, so direct your investigative effort to securing them firmly. By contrast, where guilt is inferred from many independent strands that reinforce each other (a "cable"), the strength comes from the accumulation, and no single strand needs to reach that standard on its own. The lesson: the more independent incriminating circumstances you can assemble, the more robust the case, and the less any one item must carry the whole weight. Be alert to whether your case turns on one or two pivotal facts (vulnerable) or many converging ones (stronger).
verdict: AWARENESS — this clarifies the law on jury directions and proof in circumstantial cases; it informs how you assemble and weigh evidence rather than changing a specific investigative procedure.

appealFrom: Full Court of the Supreme Court of Queensland
outcome: Appeal allowed with costs. Order of the Full Court set aside; order to review made absolute. Warrant held invalid. Appellant given liberty to apply for costs below within 14 days.
weight: High Court of Australia, unanimous seven-judge bench. Binding and a leading authority on the meaning of "reasonable grounds" for suspicion and belief and on search warrant validity.
oneLine: A search warrant is invalid unless the sworn complaint itself sets out facts establishing reasonable grounds for both the suspicion and the belief the statute requires.
whatHappened: On 22/08/1989 Detective Sergeant ROCKETT of the Office of the Special Prosecutor applied to a stipendiary magistrate for a search warrant under s 679(b) of The Criminal Code (Qld). He sought a bundle of A4 pages in the handwriting of Sir Terence LEWIS (a former Police Commissioner facing two perjury charges) plus an annotated transcript of Commission of Inquiry evidence, said to have been sent to LEWIS's solicitors. ROCKETT put before the magistrate a sworn complaint, an unsworn statutory declaration and pro forma documents, and answered the magistrate's questions. The magistrate issued the warrant; documents were seized at the solicitor GEORGE's office and sealed unopened. GEORGE obtained an order to review under s 209 of the Justices Act (Qld). The Full Court discharged the order to review. GEORGE appealed to the High Court.
whatHeld: The High Court unanimously allowed the appeal and held the warrant invalid. Strict compliance with the statutory conditions for issuing a search warrant is required, because such warrants authorise invasion of privacy and property that the common law has long protected. "Reasonable grounds" requires actual facts sufficient to induce the relevant state of mind in a reasonable person — it must appear to the issuing justice, not merely the applicant, that those grounds exist. The justice must give real attention and stand between police and citizen, not act as a rubber stamp. Suspicion and belief are different states of mind: suspicion is a positive feeling of actual apprehension or mistrust on slender material; belief requires grounds pointing more clearly to the subject matter. Critically, the sworn complaint itself must contain sufficient facts to found the reasonable suspicion and belief — unsworn material (the statutory declaration) and oral answers cannot supply the deficiency. Here the complaint established reasonable grounds for suspecting the documents were at the solicitor's office, but contained no facts showing reasonable grounds for believing they would afford evidence of the perjury offences — it contained only ROCKETT's bare assertion of his own belief. The Court also held the description of the object of a search delimits the scope of seizure: the wider the description, the harder to justify that it will afford evidence and the more the seizure power is unjustifiably broadened. The Court declined to adjourn to allow a fresh complaint.
whatItMeans: This is a foundational authority on search warrants and applies directly in WA, where s 711 of The Criminal Code (WA) and s 10 of the Crimes Act 1914 (Cth) are in substantially the same terms. When you swear an application, every fact relied on must be in the sworn document itself. Do not rely on a covering statutory declaration, briefing notes, or what you tell the issuing officer verbally — anything not sworn is immaterial and cannot save a defective application. State the actual grounds, not just a conclusion that you hold a suspicion or belief. A bare assertion that "there are reasonable grounds for believing" is fatal; you must set out the facts that make the belief reasonable to an outside reader. Distinguish clearly between what you suspect (e.g. that the thing exists or is at the location) and what you believe (that the thing will afford evidence of the offence) and supply facts for each. Describe the thing sought as narrowly and specifically as the evidence allows — link it to the particular offence. An over-broad description (e.g. "all books and records") will not be supported and exposes the warrant to challenge. Remember evidence can be inculpatory and exculpatory at once and still "afford evidence"; but material that only exculpates does not justify a warrant. A written, fact-rich sworn application is your best protection against later invalidity.
verdict: ACTION — it governs how you draft and swear every search warrant application: facts must be in the sworn complaint, suspicion and belief separately grounded, and the thing precisely described.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of Western Australia
outcome: Special leave to appeal granted; appeal allowed; order of the Court of Criminal Appeal of WA set aside; convictions quashed; new trial ordered.
weight: High Court of Australia, unanimous five-judge bench — binding and a leading authority. Note that the specific statute considered (s 36BE) has been repealed and replaced.
oneLine: Longman v The Queen establishes that where there is a long delay before a sexual complaint, a jury must be warned that the accused has lost the means of testing the allegation, making it dangerous to convict on uncorroborated evidence without careful scrutiny.
whatHappened: John Henry LONGMAN was charged in the District Court of WA with two counts of unlawfully and indecently dealing with his step-daughter (D.R.T.), a girl under 14, contrary to s 183 of the Criminal Code (WA). Count one alleged an offence between 22/02/1962 and 22/02/1963 when she was about six; count two between 22/02/1966 and 22/02/1967 when she was about ten. The complainant said she awoke on each occasion to find LONGMAN touching her genitalia, and pretended to remain asleep. She first complained to police in August 1987 — over 25 years after the first incident and over 21 years after the second. There was no corroborating evidence. LONGMAN denied the conduct and led good character evidence; police gave unchallenged evidence of an effective partial admission. He was convicted on 21/10/1988 and fined $2,000 on each count. The Court of Criminal Appeal of WA dismissed his appeal. Defence counsel had asked the trial judge for a warning under s 36BE of the Evidence Act 1906 (WA), which the judge declined to give.
whatHeld: The High Court (BRENNAN, DEANE, DAWSON, TOOHEY and McHUGH JJ) unanimously allowed the appeal. Section 36BE(1)(a) abolished the old rule of practice requiring a warning that complainants in sexual cases are a suspect class, and (b) prohibited that warning unless justified. But the section did not remove the trial judge's general duty to warn the jury of any perceptible risk of miscarriage of justice arising from the particular circumstances of the case. The joint judgment held that the long delay (more than twenty years) had necessarily impaired the fairness of the trial because LONGMAN had lost the means of testing the allegations that would have existed had complaint been made promptly. The jury should have been told that, because the complainant's evidence could not be adequately tested after such a delay, it would be dangerous to convict on that evidence alone unless, after scrutinising it with great care, considering the circumstances relevant to its evaluation, and heeding the warning, they were satisfied of its truth and accuracy. The absence of such a warning left the convictions unsafe and unsatisfactory. DEANE J emphasised the risk that the long passage of time could harden childhood fantasy or semi-fantasy into a conviction of reality; McHUGH J emphasised the fallibility of human recollection over time and the defence's lost opportunity to explore surrounding circumstances.
whatItMeans: This is the source of the "Longman warning" — a direction you will frequently encounter in delayed-complaint sexual offence cases. For your casework it means that delay between the alleged offence and the complaint is a serious evidentiary issue, not just a credibility point. The longer the delay, the more the accused loses the ability to test the allegation and gather contradicting evidence. When you investigate historical or delayed sexual offences, you should work hard to (1) pin down dates, locations and surrounding circumstances as precisely as possible, and (2) actively seek any independent corroboration — contemporaneous complaints, witnesses, documents, physical evidence — because uncorroborated evidence given long after the event invites a strong judicial warning that it is dangerous to convict on it alone. Note that the legislative framework here (s 36BE) has since been repealed and replaced, and the law on warnings in sexual cases has been substantially modified by later statute; treat this case as the foundational authority on the principle rather than a current statement of the WA statutory regime. Confirm the present legislative provisions before relying on the section numbers discussed.
verdict: AWARENESS — this is foundational appellate authority on jury warnings that shapes how delayed-complaint sexual cases are run; it does not directly change your investigative powers but should drive how you gather and preserve corroborating evidence.

appealFrom: Full Court of the Supreme Court of Victoria
outcome: Appeal allowed; conviction and sentence set aside; new trial ordered.
weight: High Court of Australia, seven-member bench — the leading and binding statement of the common law of self-defence in Australia.
oneLine: The High Court restated the common law of self-defence into a single question — did the accused believe on reasonable grounds that it was necessary to do what he did — replacing the complex six-step Viro formula.
whatHappened: Fadil ZECEVIC was convicted in the Supreme Court of Victoria of murdering Harold TRIEBEL on 16/07/1983 at Ascot Vale. The two were neighbours in a block of units and had repeatedly clashed over leaving security gates open. On the day, after an argument ZECEVIC said TRIEBEL stabbed him in the chest, threatened to "blow your head off", and that he believed TRIEBEL might have a gun in his car. ZECEVIC went to his unit, fetched a gun, returned and shot TRIEBEL dead. The trial judge withdrew self-defence from the jury, finding the only open inference was that ZECEVIC did not reasonably believe he faced an unlawful attack threatening death or serious harm. The Full Court dismissed his appeal. He appealed to the High Court, which convened a seven-member bench to review the law of self-defence as stated in Viro v The Queen.
whatHeld: The Court (Wilson, Dawson and Toohey JJ, with Mason CJ, Brennan, Deane and Gaudron JJ agreeing in result) allowed the appeal, quashed the conviction and ordered a new trial. It held the elaborate six-proposition formula from Viro created difficult, confusing directions for trial judges and juries and should be abandoned. The law was restated simply: the ultimate question is whether the accused believed on reasonable grounds that it was necessary in self-defence to do what he did — if he had that belief and there were reasonable grounds for it, or the jury is left in reasonable doubt, he is acquitted. The Court confirmed self-defence has an objective element (reasonable grounds) and rejected the argument that an honest belief alone suffices. The onus remains on the Crown to disprove self-defence beyond reasonable doubt. It also held there is no rule requiring retreat as far as possible — retreat is just one circumstance to weigh — and that an original aggressor who provokes a lawful attack cannot then claim self-defence. On the facts, self-defence was squarely raised and should have been left to the jury; the trial judge wrongly substituted his own view.
whatItMeans: This is the leading common law authority on self-defence in Australia, but note WA self-defence is governed by Criminal Code (WA) s 248, not the common law — the judgment itself flags the code states operate differently. The case is still important for understanding the framework. The practical lesson for your casework: where self-defence may arise, the burden falls on the prosecution to disprove it, so build your brief to address it. Gather evidence on the accused's belief and whether it was reasonable — the nature and immediacy of any threat, proportionality of force, opportunity to retreat or break off, who started the conflict, and the accused's account. Here the prosecution's strongest point was the time gap: the accused left, armed himself, and returned. Evidence of a delay, of the accused arming himself, of an aggressor returning to a finished conflict, and of staged scenes (the prosecution alleged a knife was planted in the deceased's hand) are all critical to disproving self-defence.
verdict: AWARENESS for WA — this is foundational common law on self-defence, but WA charges run under Criminal Code (WA) s 248, so treat it as background to how courts reason rather than a directly governing test.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of the Supreme Court of Tasmania
outcome: Special leave granted; appeal allowed; Court of Criminal Appeal's order for retrial set aside; verdicts of acquittal on the 26 counts restored.
weight: High Court of Australia, five Justices — the leading authority on the limits of detention after arrest at common law.
oneLine: WILLIAMS v THE QUEEN confirms there is no power to detain an arrested person for questioning or further investigation — they must be brought before a justice as soon as practicable.
whatHappened: WILLIAMS was arrested near Scottsdale, Tasmania at about 6 a.m. on 17/05/1984 for burglary and stealing offences committed at Scottsdale (counts 27 to 29). He was taken to Launceston where, after being shown police reports, he indicated involvement in other offences across northern Tasmania (counts 1 to 26). From about 1.10 p.m. detectives conducted a series of interviews in which he confessed to all the offences; the last interview ended about 8.30 p.m. He confirmed the records to a police inspector at 9.03 p.m. and was taken before a magistrate the next day. The trial judge found WILLIAMS could have been charged with the Scottsdale matters and brought before a court by 2.15 p.m. on 17/05/1984, so his detention after that time was unlawful. The judge excluded the confessions on the 26 other counts in his discretion, the Crown offered no evidence and WILLIAMS was acquitted on those counts. The Court of Criminal Appeal allowed the Attorney-General's appeal and ordered a retrial. WILLIAMS sought special leave to the High Court.
whatHeld: The High Court granted special leave, allowed the appeal and restored the acquittals. It held that there is no power to detain an arrested person for the purpose of questioning or further investigation, whether about the offence of arrest or other offences. The statutory obligations under Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 34A(1) ('as soon as is practicable') and Criminal Code 1924 (Tas) s 303(1) ('without delay') reflect the common law: an arrested person may be detained only for the purpose of being brought before a justice as soon as reasonably practicable. Reasonable time is allowed for completing necessary inquiries, formulating and laying charges and preparing paperwork, but detention may not be prolonged to provide an opportunity for interrogation or evidence-gathering. The Court rejected the English approach in Dallison v Caffery and Holgate-Mohammed v Duke, affirming R v Iorlano. Gibbs CJ held the trial judge applied too strict a test in the discretionary exclusion (a voluntary, confirmed confession is only most exceptionally excluded for illegality), but the majority upheld the acquittals because the detention was unlawful. The Court also held the Attorney-General's appeal raised a question of mixed fact and law, not 'a question of law alone' under s 401(2)(b), so the Court of Criminal Appeal had no power to grant leave.
whatItMeans: This is a foundational authority you must respect: you cannot hold an arrested person in custody so you can question them or continue investigating — the detention clock runs only for the time reasonably needed to do the paperwork, formulate charges and get them before a justice or magistrate. Questioning is not itself unlawful, and you may question while genuinely processing someone toward court, but you must not delay bringing them before a justice in order to interview them — including about additional offences they volunteer. Once a reasonable time to charge and present has passed, continued detention is unlawful and confessions obtained during it are at risk of exclusion. Note this case predates modern WA detention regimes — your actual time limits and questioning powers come from current WA legislation, not this judgment. Treat WILLIAMS as the common law backdrop explaining why detention-for-questioning powers must be expressly authorised and time-limited by statute. Build your timeline so charging and court presentation are not held up by interviews, and document why any delay was genuinely about process, not interrogation.
verdict: AWARENESS — a landmark statement of principle on detention after arrest, but WA now has its own statutory detention and questioning regime, so check current legislation for your operative time limits.

caseName: He Kaw Teh v The Queen
appealFrom: Full Court of the Supreme Court of Victoria (Court of Criminal Appeal)
outcome: Special leave granted; appeal allowed; judgment of the Victorian Full Court set aside; matter remitted to that Court to proceed in accordance with the High Court's judgment.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-justice bench — a foundational and binding authority on mens rea in statutory offences. Frequently cited and still the leading Australian statement on the presumption of mental elements.
oneLine: The High Court held that Customs Act 1901 drug importation and possession offences require the prosecution to prove the accused knew of the existence and nature of the narcotic goods — they are not absolute offences.
whatHappened: HE KAW TEH flew from Kuala Lumpur to Melbourne on 20/10/1982. Customs found 2.788kg of heroin in a false bottom of his bag. He was charged under s 233B(1)(b) of the Customs Act 1901 (Cth) with importing heroin, and under s 233B(1)(c) with possession of imported heroin. The trial judge directed the jury that the Crown need prove no state of mind, and that the accused bore the onus of proving honest and reasonable belief (on the importation count) or reasonable excuse (on the possession count). He was convicted. The Victorian Full Court dismissed his appeal because it was bound by R v Parsons and R v Ditroia and Tucci. He sought special leave to the High Court.
whatHeld: The Court (Gibbs CJ, Mason, Wilson, Brennan and Dawson JJ) granted leave, allowed the appeal and remitted the matter. There is a strong presumption that mens rea is an element of every statutory offence, displaced only by clear words or necessary implication, and the presumption is strongest for serious, truly criminal offences. Given the gravity (life imprisonment), the Court held that on a charge under s 233B(1)(b) the prosecution must prove the accused knew he was importing narcotic goods (knowledge includes wilful blindness). On s 233B(1)(c), possession itself imports knowledge — a person does not possess narcotic goods they do not know exist, so the Crown must prove the accused knew of the existence of the goods in their control. The onus of proving knowledge rests on the prosecution beyond reasonable doubt; the accused carries only an evidential burden. R v Bush, R v Gardiner, R v Parsons and R v Ditroia and Tucci were disapproved. On this mental-element reasoning the Court divided 4:1 — Wilson J would have treated the offences as strict liability subject to an honest-and-reasonable-mistake defence, rather than requiring the Crown to prove knowledge as part of its case — but the orders (leave granted, appeal allowed, remitted) were unanimous. The trial directions were wrong; the matter was returned to the Full Court to consider whether the proviso (no substantial miscarriage) applied.
whatItMeans: For drug importation and possession matters, knowledge is a live element you must prove. It is not enough to show the accused physically carried or controlled a bag containing drugs — you must be able to establish they knew the drugs were there (and, for importation, knew they were importing narcotic goods). Build your brief around proof of knowledge. The judgments make clear knowledge can be inferred from the circumstances — concealment, weight, the nature and feel of the container, suspicious circumstances of handing over, and an absence of any convincing innocent explanation. Wilful blindness counts as knowledge: where a courier deliberately avoids inquiry despite aroused suspicions, that can satisfy the mental element. Gather evidence going to what the accused knew or deliberately ignored — records of how the bag came to them, their conduct and statements, the obviousness of the concealment. Mere custody is not the finish line.
verdict: ACTION — this changes what you must prove in drug import/possession cases: knowledge is an element, so your investigation must capture evidence of the accused's awareness or wilful blindness.

appealFrom: Full Court of the Federal Court of Australia (on appeal from the Supreme Court of the Northern Territory)
outcome: Special leave to appeal granted; appeal dismissed with costs. The Full Court's order quashing the convictions and directing a new trial stood.
weight: High Court of Australia, unanimous five-member Court — the leading authority on the mental element of common-law murder; binding and frequently applied.
oneLine: For reckless murder at common law, the accused must know death or grievous bodily harm is probable, not merely possible — and wilful blindness has no role.
whatHappened: In the early hours of 18/08/1983, after being ejected from a crowded motel bar near Ayers Rock, CRABBE returned at the controls of his prime mover and one trailer and drove the vehicle through the wall into the bar. Five people died and many were injured. He did nothing to help and left, and was apprehended the following day. He was tried before the Chief Justice of the Northern Territory and a jury and convicted on five counts of murder. The Criminal Code Act 1983 (NT) had not been passed at the material times, so common law governed the mental element. The trial judge gave two redirections referring to the accused foreseeing the possibility people were in the bar and to "wilful blindness". The Full Court of the Federal Court, by majority, quashed the conviction and ordered a new trial. The Crown sought special leave to appeal.
whatHeld: The High Court (Gibbs CJ, Wilson, Brennan, Deane and Dawson JJ) held it is settled law in Australia that, absent an intention to kill or do grievous bodily harm, a person who does an act knowing it is probable (i.e. likely) that death or grievous bodily harm will result is guilty of murder if death follows; knowledge of a mere possibility is not enough. The relevant element is knowledge that those consequences will probably occur, not reckless indifference as such, though indifference does not save the accused. There is no valid distinction between foresight of consequences and foresight of circumstances — the test is simply whether the accused knew his actions would probably cause death or grievous bodily harm. On wilful blindness: actual knowledge or foresight is required and imputed knowledge is not enough; deliberate abstention from inquiry is at most evidence of actual knowledge. The doctrine had no part to play here, and the trial judge's references to mere possibility were a material misdirection, and the reference to wilful blindness was also erroneous.
whatItMeans: What it means for your casework: this is the leading common-law statement of the fault element for murder, but in WA murder is governed by the Criminal Code (WA), so this decision does not directly control how you charge a WA homicide — treat it as background to the concepts of foresight and recklessness. The enduring lesson is the sharp line between "probable" and "possible": where you are building a case that an accused foresaw death or serious harm, your evidence must point to the accused knowing the result was likely, not merely conceivable. Do not rely on "wilful blindness" as a shortcut to proving knowledge — actual knowledge or foresight must be proved, and an accused's deliberate failure to check (e.g. not looking before driving in) is only circumstantial evidence of what was actually in his mind, not a substitute for it. Gather and record what the suspect knew, saw and was told before acting.
verdict: AWARENESS only — a foundational common-law murder authority, but WA homicide is charged under the Criminal Code (WA), so it does not change how you frame charges here.

appealFrom: Full Court of the Federal Court of Australia
outcome: Special leave granted; appeals dismissed (by majority). Convictions stood at this stage. Murphy and Deane JJ dissenting.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-judge bench; landmark and frequently cited authority on circumstantial evidence and the proof of facts founding inferences of guilt.
oneLine: In dismissing the CHAMBERLAIN appeals, the majority confirmed how circumstantial evidence must be reasoned and proved — each fact founding an inference of guilt must itself be established beyond reasonable doubt.
whatHappened: Alice Lynne CHAMBERLAIN was convicted of murdering her nine-week-old baby Azaria at Ayers Rock on 17/08/1980; Michael Leigh CHAMBERLAIN was convicted as accessory after the fact. The Crown case was wholly circumstantial: alleged throat-cutting in the family car during a 5-10 minute absence, blood and claimed foetal haemoglobin in the car, the cut and vegetation-rubbed condition of the baby's jumpsuit, and alleged inconsistencies in Mrs CHAMBERLAIN's accounts of seeing a dingo. The defence asserted a dingo took the baby, supported by independent campers (notably Mrs LOWE, who said she heard the baby cry after the alleged time of death), dingo tracks and drag marks, and evidence of aggressive dingoes in the area. The Federal Court dismissed the appeals. They sought special leave to the High Court.
whatHeld: The Court granted special leave but, by majority (Gibbs CJ and Mason J, Brennan J), dismissed the appeals. The Court held that the Federal Court does have power to set aside a verdict as unsafe or unsatisfactory (overruling Duff on that point). On circumstantial reasoning the Court held that the jury must consider all the evidence together and may draw an inference of guilt from a combination of facts none of which alone would support it, but that a jury cannot treat a fact as the basis for an inference of guilt unless satisfied of that fact beyond reasonable doubt, and that in a criminal case the circumstances must exclude any reasonable hypothesis consistent with innocence. The Court also held it is wrong as a general rule to direct a jury to examine each item of evidence separately and discard it unless satisfied of it beyond reasonable doubt at that stage. The majority concluded that, even setting aside the unsafe foetal-blood evidence, the remaining evidence entitled the jury safely to convict. Murphy J and Deane J dissented, finding the verdicts unsafe and unsatisfactory.
whatItMeans: This is a foundational authority on circumstantial evidence that shapes how you build and present a brief in a no-confession, no-direct-evidence case. Every fact you rely on as a building block for an inference of guilt must itself be capable of being proved beyond reasonable doubt — if a key fact (here, that the blood was foetal) cannot safely be established, it cannot be used as a foundation for the inference, though it may still show lesser matters (such as that blood was present). Build your case so the combined circumstances exclude every reasonable hypothesis consistent with innocence; identify and confront the innocent explanations, because a single fact inconsistent with guilt can destroy a circumstantial case. Be alert that contested forensic/scientific evidence is for the tribunal of fact to weigh, but where reliability is genuinely doubtful, do not let the whole case hang on it. Preserve testing materials (plates, gels, samples) where possible — the destruction of samples here drew strong criticism and prevented independent re-testing.
verdict: AWARENESS — this is a high-authority statement of principle on proof of circumstantial evidence that informs how you assemble briefs, not a rule changing arrest or charge procedure.

appealFrom: Supreme Court of South Australia (Court of Criminal Appeal)
outcome: Special leave to appeal granted; appeal allowed; convictions set aside; new trial ordered.
weight: High persuasive and precedential weight — a five-member High Court decision settling the application of Bunning v Cross to confessional evidence, though the judges differed in reasoning and the convictions were ultimately quashed on the misdirection ground.
oneLine: Cleland v The Queen confirms the Bunning v Cross public-policy discretion can apply to confessional evidence (not just 'real' evidence), alongside the Lee unfairness discretion — though the Court divided on the discretion point and quashed the convictions only on a separate jury-misdirection ground.
whatHappened: CLELAND was convicted in the Supreme Court of South Australia of shop-breaking and larceny and armed robbery, arising from a gun-shop break-in on 21/03/1981 and a hotel hold-up on 26/03/1981. The case against him rested largely on an oral confession allegedly made in Melbourne, which he denied making. CLELAND and his co-accused BARTELS were arrested in Melbourne shortly after 1.00pm on 09/04/1981 and taken to Russell Street Police Station. An Adelaide CIB officer flew over and questioning began about 8.35pm, continuing most of the evening; CLELAND was charged shortly before midnight. CLELAND conceded he was not badgered, pressured, threatened or forced. It was common ground on appeal that detaining him after 5.30pm without bringing him before a justice breached s 460 Crimes Act 1958 (Vic) and rendered his detention unlawful. The trial judge wrongly held the detention lawful but said that, if wrong, he would unhesitatingly admit the confession. Separately, the trial judge misdirected the jury by suggesting CLELAND was the only possible source of details in the confession, when those details could equally have been gleaned from BARTELS who was being questioned simultaneously.
whatHeld: The Court granted special leave, allowed the appeal, quashed the convictions and ordered a new trial. On the confession point, the Court held that the principles in R v Lee (unfairness to the accused) remain entirely unaffected by Reg v Ireland and Bunning v Cross. A voluntary confession may still be excluded if its reception would be unfair to the accused. The general Bunning v Cross public-policy discretion to exclude unlawfully or improperly obtained evidence does apply to confessional evidence, not just "real" evidence, although confessions are its less common area of operation. On the facts the Court divided: Gibbs CJ and Wilson J held the confession was rightly admitted and the first ground failed; Deane and Dawson JJ considered the trial judge should also have addressed the distinct unfairness discretion (Deane J treating that point as strictly unnecessary to the result). The Court stressed that rejection of a voluntary confession on the public-policy ground alone is most exceptional — Gibbs CJ agreed with Brennan J in Collins that it is difficult to conceive of a voluntary confession fairly admissible against an accused being rejected in the public interest. The convictions were quashed unanimously on a separate ground — the jury misdirection (the third ground) caused a miscarriage of justice (see Simic v The Queen).
whatItMeans: When you obtain a confession, be alert to two separate exclusionary risks if any illegality or impropriety attended the questioning or detention: first, that admitting it would be unfair to the accused (reliability/fair-trial focus); second, the broader public-policy concern that courts should not reward unlawful police conduct. The single most important operational lesson is to bring an arrested person before a justice or court as soon as practicable and not use detention as an opportunity for prolonged questioning. Several judges signalled that a confession obtained during unlawful detention will commonly be excluded on public-policy grounds unless the illegality is slight or technical. Even where the suspect concedes he was not pressured, threatened or forced, unlawful detention can still ground exclusion. Document the lawfulness of arrest and detention carefully, record when and why an arrestee was held, and ensure prompt presentation to a court. Note also the practical evidentiary trap exposed by the misdirection: where co-accused are questioned simultaneously and detectives share information, you cannot assume a detail in a confession could only have come from the accused — the defence can point to the co-accused as the source. Build corroboration beyond the contested confession wherever possible.
verdict: AWARENESS — this is an authoritative High Court statement of confession-exclusion principles that shapes how a confession will be tested in court, but the binding rule turns on observing lawful arrest/detention procedures you should already follow.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of Victoria
outcome: Special leave to appeal granted; appeal dismissed. Conviction upheld (3:2).
weight: High Court of Australia — strong, foundational authority on identification evidence. Note the majority reasoning differed: Gibbs CJ and Mason J both upheld admissibility but Mason J was the more influential, treating out-of-court identification as inherently reliable original evidence. Stephen and Murphy JJ dissented, favouring exclusion of post-detection photo-identification. Decided in 1981 and predates uniform evidence legislation; treat as the common-law baseline and check it against current WA law and policy.
oneLine: The High Court held that evidence of identification made from police photographs is legally admissible, but using photo-boards after a suspect is arrested or firmly suspected (instead of an identification parade) is improper and may be excluded as more prejudicial than probative.
whatHappened: ALEXANDER was convicted in Victoria of entering a building as a trespasser with intent to steal (a National Bank burglary at Belgrave on 15/06/1977). The key issue was identification. Several witnesses first identified him by picking his photograph from a folder of about a dozen police photographs. Constable BEALE made his identification before ALEXANDER was arrested (during the investigation). Constable WILLIAMS, Mrs FEDELE and Mr CONNELL identified the photograph after ALEXANDER was already in custody. No identification parade was ever held and no valid reason was given for not holding one. CONNELL could not recall in court which photograph he had picked, but said he had identified one and recorded it in a statement; Detective Sergeant DUGGAN gave evidence that the photograph CONNELL picked was ALEXANDER's. The Victorian Court of Criminal Appeal dismissed his appeal. He sought special leave to the High Court.
whatHeld: Special leave granted; appeal dismissed by majority (Gibbs CJ, Mason and Aickin JJ; Stephen and Murphy JJ dissenting). The Court held: (1) evidence of an out-of-court identification made by means of police photographs is admissible as a matter of law — it goes to weight, not admissibility. (2) The trial judge always retains a discretion to exclude such evidence where its prejudicial effect outweighs its probative value, particularly the 'rogues' gallery' risk that the jury will infer the accused has a police record. (3) It is 'most undesirable' (Gibbs CJ) and deserving of 'strong censure' (Mason J) to use photo-identification once a person is arrested or firmly suspected and an identification parade could be held — the parade is the safer, fairer method because the accused can observe it. A distinction was drawn between using photos in the detection process (acceptable) and after a suspect is known — though the sharper view that post-detection photo-ID should be excluded was the dissent's (Stephen and Murphy JJ); the majority censured the practice but upheld admission. (4) On third-party proof of an earlier identification: where the identifying witness testifies that he made an earlier identification but cannot now recall the details, another person (e.g. a police officer) may give evidence of which photograph or person was picked — this is original evidence, not hearsay. But if the witness does not testify to having made the earlier identification, the third party's evidence of it is inadmissible hearsay (applying Christie, Teper, Sparks). Despite criticising the police failure to hold a parade, the majority found the conviction safe on the whole of the evidence.
whatItMeans: This is foundational authority on how you should gather identification evidence. Once you have arrested or firmly suspect a person, you should hold a properly conducted identification parade wherever possible — not show witnesses a photo-board. Photo-boards are for the detection stage, when you do not yet know who you are looking for. If you resort to photographs after you have a firm suspect with no good reason, you risk the identification evidence being excluded at trial as more prejudicial than probative, and you invite judicial censure. If you must use photographs, take precautions: avoid prejudicial images (no prison clothing, handcuffs), use a fair spread of similar-looking people, and never show a single photo before a parade. Be alert to the 'displacement' effect — once a witness sees a photo, that image can replace their original memory and taint any later parade or dock identification, so record exactly when and how each identification was made. On documenting identifications: get the identifying witness to record their own identification at the time (e.g. note on the photo or signed statement). Your evidence as the observing officer of what a witness picked is only useful if that witness later testifies they made the identification — if the witness cannot or will not say they made it, your account of it is hearsay and worthless in court. Note: this is a Victorian/HCA decision; check current WA procedure and policy for identification parades and photo-boards.
verdict: ACTION — it sets out how identification evidence must be obtained: use parades once a suspect is firmly identified, reserve photo-boards for the detection stage, and document identifications correctly to avoid exclusion at trial.

outcome: Established the discretion to exclude improperly/illegally obtained evidence
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: Courts have a discretion to exclude improperly or illegally obtained evidence, weighing the public interest in convicting the guilty against the public interest in not condoning unlawful police conduct.
whatHappened: The case concerned evidence obtained through an improper/unlawful exercise of police power; the High Court set out how courts decide whether to admit or exclude such evidence. (Held at principle level here.)
whatHeld: There is a public-policy discretion to exclude evidence that was illegally or improperly obtained — distinct from the unfairness discretion — balancing the public interest in convicting the guilty against the public interest in not condoning unlawful conduct by police. Stephen and Aickin JJ delivered the leading reasons (Barwick CJ, Jacobs and Murphy JJ also sat; Murphy J dissenting), and stressed this discretion is not primarily about fairness to the accused — that is a separate, more general discretion — but a weighing of two competing public interests. (Companion: R v Ireland (1970) 126 CLR 321.)
whatItMeans: Get the power right the first time. An unlawful search, entry or seizure hands the defence the Bunning v Cross argument to exclude your evidence. Record your authority for every act — which power, what grounds, when — so the balance falls your way if it’s litigated.
verdict: Drives the habit: lawful power + recorded authority for every search, entry and seizure.

court: Supreme Court of the Northern Territory
outcome: Established the Anunga Rules for interviewing Aboriginal suspects
weight: NT Supreme Court — strong persuasive weight in WA
oneLine: Guidelines for interviewing Aboriginal suspects — an interpreter where needed, a prisoner’s friend, and explaining the caution phrase by phrase with the suspect repeating it back.
whatHappened: The NT Supreme Court laid down practical guidelines (the ‘Anunga Rules’) for the fair interviewing of Aboriginal suspects. (Held at principle level here.)
whatHeld: When interviewing an Aboriginal suspect: use an interpreter where there is any doubt about language; have a prisoner's friend present where practicable; explain the caution phrase by phrase and have the suspect say it back in their own words; take care over offers of food, drink and rest.
whatItMeans: Pilbara-critical for you. Build the Anunga Rules into every relevant interview in your patch — interpreter, prisoner's friend, caution explained and confirmed — or the admissions are at real risk of exclusion. Anunga is a Northern Territory Supreme Court decision: persuasive, not binding, in WA (where admissibility runs on the Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA) and the common law) — but it carries strong weight and breach puts admissions at real risk.
verdict: Directly changes interview practice in your region — Anunga compliance, every relevant job.

appealFrom: Supreme Court of the Northern Territory
outcome: Appeal allowed in part: murder conviction and sentence set aside; verdict of guilty of manslaughter substituted under s 47(4) of the Northern Territory Supreme Court Act 1961 (Cth); matter remitted to the Supreme Court of the Northern Territory for sentence. Menzies J dissented as to the substituted verdict.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-member bench — a leading and frequently cited authority on the trial judge's duty to direct on all open issues and on foresight as the mental element of reckless murder. Majority of four (Barwick CJ, McTiernan, Windeyer and Owen JJ on quashing; Owen J differing on outcome), with Menzies J dissenting on the order.
oneLine: PEMBLE v THE QUEEN confirms that a trial judge must direct the jury on every defence and verdict open on the evidence, even where defence counsel has abandoned them, and that murder by recklessness requires actual foresight of death or grievous bodily harm.
whatHappened: On 25/09/1970 at Darwin, PEMBLE killed Josephine CROSBIE, who had recently ended their relationship. He sawed down a single-shot .22 rifle so he could approach her from behind, cocked it, and came up behind her as she sat on the mudguard of a car outside the Fannie Bay Hotel. He said he only meant to frighten her, did not know it was loaded, called her name, stumbled, and the rifle discharged into the back of her head. Crown eye-witnesses said he walked up, pointed the rifle at the back of her head and shot her, with no stumble or cry. At trial defence counsel expressly abandoned any acquittal and asked the jury only to convict of manslaughter rather than murder. The trial judge gave a very brief summing up, largely endorsing counsel's definitions, and the jury convicted of murder.
whatHeld: The High Court set aside the murder conviction. A trial judge must direct the jury fully and accurately on every matter the jury could properly find on the evidence — including defences and lesser verdicts — even where defence counsel has not relied on them or has expressly abandoned them. The summing up was defective on murder by reckless indifference: it was not enough to use the words "reckless" or "reckless indifference". The jury had to be told the accused must have had actual foresight that death or grievous bodily harm was a possible (Barwick CJ) or probable/likely (McTiernan J, Menzies J) consequence, with that foresight to be inferred from all the circumstances including age, background, emotional state and sobriety. The majority (Barwick CJ, McTiernan and Windeyer JJ) held that on PEMBLE's own account the killing could not be excusable accident or misadventure — brandishing a loaded, cocked, sawn-off rifle within feet of the victim was an unlawful and dangerous act (Barwick CJ put it as at least an attempted assault; McTiernan J grounded it in the firearms ordinance) making the killing manslaughter — so no acquittal was open and, by a 3:2 majority, a verdict of manslaughter was substituted under s 47(4). Menzies J dissented on the substituted verdict (favouring a new trial); Owen J considered acquittal on both charges was open because the victim, turned away, may not have apprehended the threat (no assault).
whatItMeans: This is a foundational case on the judge's duty to leave all viable defences and verdicts to the jury, but for your work the key lesson is about the mental element for murder by recklessness. When you build a homicide brief that relies on recklessness rather than a clear intent to kill or do grievous bodily harm, you must gather evidence going to the suspect's actual state of mind — that he foresaw death or really serious harm as a real possibility and went ahead anyway. A mere objective "reasonable person would have known it was dangerous" is not enough for murder; that level points to manslaughter. Document everything bearing on foresight: what the suspect knew about the weapon being loaded, how he handled it, his words, his sobriety, emotional state, background and any admissions. Note WA is a Criminal Code jurisdiction, so the precise elements of murder, manslaughter and accident here are governed by NT/common-law principles and may not map directly onto the WA Criminal Code — treat this case as guidance on the foresight/recklessness distinction and on the breadth of issues that can arise at trial, not as a statement of WA Code elements.
verdict: AWARENESS — it sharpens how you evidence the mental element in a recklessness-based homicide, but the binding elements in WA come from the Code, so it informs rather than directly governs your charging.

appealFrom: Supreme Court of South Australia (Court of Criminal Appeal)
outcome: Special leave to appeal refused with costs; the order for a new trial stood. (Editor's note: on retrial IRELAND was convicted of murder on 27/08/1970 and his further appeal was dismissed.)
weight: High Court of Australia, unanimous (McTiernan, Windeyer, Owen and Walsh JJ all agreeing with Barwick CJ). A leading authority on the public-policy discretion to exclude improperly obtained evidence.
oneLine: The High Court refused the Crown special leave, confirming that evidence obtained by unlawful or unfair means is not automatically excluded but is subject to a judicial discretion weighing the public interest in conviction against protection from unlawful treatment.
whatHappened: A female hotel licensee was stabbed to death in Adelaide on 16/11/1969. IRELAND, who when questioned denied having been at the hotel on the evening of the killing, became the suspect after his footprints and a cigarette butt were found near the body. On 22/11/1969 police interrogated him; after a caution he said he did not wish to answer further questions, but the officer persisted with a series of questions to which he repeatedly said "I don't wish to answer". Police later compelled him to have his scratched right hand photographed, and a doctor examined the hand, linking the marks to a knife with a broken handle. The trial judge admitted the questioning evidence, the photographs and the medical evidence over objection. IRELAND was convicted of murder. The South Australian Court of Criminal Appeal set aside the conviction by majority and ordered a new trial. The Attorney-General of South Australia sought special leave to appeal to the High Court.
whatHeld: Special leave refused; an appeal would have failed. The High Court held that evidence of relevant facts or things obtained by unlawful or unfair acts is not for that reason alone inadmissible — whether the unlawfulness derives from common law or statute — but whenever such unlawfulness or unfairness appears the judge has a discretion to reject the evidence and must consider its exercise, weighing the public need to convict offenders against the public interest in protecting the individual from unlawful and unfair treatment ("convictions obtained by the aid of unlawful or unfair acts may be obtained at too high a price"). On the facts: the account of the persisted questioning after the caution was irrelevant and inadmissible because the accused's failure to answer after warning could not amount to an admission. Compelling a person to submit to photography for a purpose other than identification was beyond police power at common law and under s. 81 of the Police Offences Act 1953-1960 (SA); the photographs and dependent medical evidence should have been excluded as a matter of discretion, the trial judge having failed to exercise any discretion at all. An aggregate of faults, none fatal alone, can together mean the trial miscarried.
whatItMeans: This is a foundational case on illegally or improperly obtained evidence. What it means for your casework: gathering evidence unlawfully will not automatically destroy a brief, but it puts the evidence at the mercy of a judicial discretion that weighs your conduct against the accused's rights — so do it properly the first time. Once a suspect has been cautioned and says he does not wish to answer, stop interrogating; persisting is improper and his silence proves nothing and is inadmissible. You cannot compel a suspect to be photographed or examined for any purpose beyond establishing identity unless a statute authorises it and you meet its conditions — here breaching the procedural safeguards denied the accused his own medical examination and got the evidence excluded. An officer's personal opinion that the suspect committed the crime is irrelevant and prejudicial. Confirm modern WA equivalents for compelled photography, forensic procedures and silence before relying on this.
verdict: AWARENESS-level but practically important: it does not change a single procedure but sets the discretionary framework that governs whether your unlawfully obtained evidence survives — investigate lawfully so it does.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal of Queensland (Reg. v. Plomp [1962] Qd. R. 161)
outcome: Application for special leave to appeal refused; conviction for wilful murder stands.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-judge bench (Dixon CJ, Kitto, Taylor, Menzies and Windeyer JJ). Leading and frequently cited authority on motive and circumstantial evidence.
oneLine: PLOMP v The Queen confirms that motive and surrounding conduct are part of the circumstantial proof of guilt, not a separate add-on requiring the killing to be proved first.
whatHappened: PLOMP was convicted of the wilful murder of his wife, Fay Irene PLOMP, who drowned while surfing alone with him at dusk at Southport on 24/02/1961. There were no independent witnesses; the only accounts of the drowning came from PLOMP himself. The surf was not dangerous and the deceased was a good swimmer. Her body was found about half a mile south of where he said they had been bathing, with the bathing costume straps unbuttoned and only a superficial forehead abrasion. The Crown case was wholly circumstantial. Beyond the drowning itself, evidence showed PLOMP had formed a relationship with Herma KONING, told her his wife was dead, introduced her to a child as their "new mummy" before the death, sought to marry her days after the death, lied about the relationship and tried to get her to lie to police, and said he could end the inquest by claiming privilege as the only witness. He sought special leave to appeal to the High Court.
whatHeld: Special leave refused. The Court rejected three arguments. First, there is no special rule giving a court of criminal appeal a free hand simply because evidence is circumstantial; the question is whether it was open to the jury, properly directed, to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that guilt was the only rational inference. Second, and most importantly, motive and surrounding conduct are not a separate category that can only be used after the killing is independently proved. In a circumstantial case, no part of the circumstances can be set aside as relating to "motive only". Evidence of motive and conduct, in words Menzies J adopted from Lord Atkinson in R v Ball, "necessarily goes to prove the fact of the homicide by the accused" — it is more probable that people are killed by those with a motive than by those without. All the circumstances must be weighed together as a single body of proof. Third, the rule that circumstantial evidence must exclude every reasonable hypothesis but guilt is not separate from proof beyond reasonable doubt — it flows from that standard. The trial direction (that motive could not be used until death was shown to be non-accidental and caused by him) actually went too far in the accused's favour, but caused no prejudice.
whatItMeans: When you build a circumstantial case, treat motive, relationship history, lies, and post-offence conduct as part of the proof that the accused committed the act — not as a bolt-on that only matters once you have separately proved a killing. The whole package is weighed together. Here, the surrounding evidence (the affair, calling the wife dead before she died, immediate moves to remarry, lying and pressuring a witness, attempts to shut down the inquest) is what lifted an ambiguous drowning into a sustainable murder verdict. Gather and document the full picture: the accused's relationships, financial or personal inducements, statements before and after, and any lies or attempts to influence witnesses. Remember the flip side the Court noted — absence of motive can point toward accident, so the presence of a strong motive is genuinely probative. But note the warning: motive alone is never enough to convict; you still need circumstances from which guilt is the only rational inference.
verdict: AWARENESS — this is a foundational High Court statement on how circumstantial evidence and motive work together; it shapes how you assemble and present a brief but does not change a specific procedure.

appealFrom: Full Court of the Supreme Court of New South Wales
outcome: Appeal allowed with costs; order of the Full Court of the Supreme Court of NSW set aside; verdict set aside and a new trial ordered.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-judge bench; a leading and constantly applied authority on inferences from a party's failure to give evidence or call a witness.
oneLine: Jones v Dunkel establishes that an unexplained failure by a party to give evidence or call a witness allows the tribunal to more confidently draw inferences already open against that party on the evidence.
whatHappened: A truck driver, JONES, was killed on 15/01/1953 on the Hume Highway about 12 miles south of Berrima when his northbound International truck collided with a southbound diesel truck driven by HEGEDUS, an employee of the defendant DUNKEL. His widow sued for negligence under the Compensation to Relatives Act 1897-1946 (NSW). The evidence about the cause of the collision was circumstantial and meagre: the positions of the vehicles, the damage, the wet road and a curve. HEGEDUS was not called to give evidence; only a signed statement he made in hospital three days later was tendered. At trial the defendants' counsel sought a directed verdict and announced he would call no evidence. The jury found for the defendants. A juryman had asked the trial judge whether HEGEDUS's failure to give evidence could be treated as a weakness in the defence, and the High Court found the judge's answer to that question incomplete.
whatHeld: By majority (KITTO, MENZIES and WINDEYER JJ; DIXON CJ and TAYLOR J dissenting), the appeal was allowed and a new trial ordered. The Court held there was sufficient evidence to leave the question of negligence to the jury. More importantly, it held that the trial judge had misdirected the jury on the significance of HEGEDUS's absence from the witness box. The principle: where a party fails, without explanation, to give evidence or call a witness who could be expected to assist their case, the tribunal may infer that the evidence would not have helped that party, and any inference already open against that party on the evidence may be drawn more confidently. The Court was clear this cannot fill gaps in a case or convert mere suspicion or conjecture into inference — it only strengthens inferences for which there is already an evidentiary basis. The Court also reaffirmed that a circumstantial case requires a more probable inference, not a mere choice between competing guesses of equal probability.
whatItMeans: This is one of the most frequently cited evidence cases in Australia and you will meet it constantly. When a suspect or party who could explain the facts stays silent or fails to call an obvious witness, and there is no good explanation, the unexplained absence strengthens the inferences already available against them. For your casework this means: build your circumstantial case so that the inferences you need are genuinely more probable on the evidence, not just one guess among several. The rule does not let you fill evidentiary gaps with someone's silence — you still need a positive foundation. Be aware that in criminal trials the operation of this principle is heavily restricted, especially as against an accused who has a right to silence; this judgment arose in a civil negligence claim, so apply it with care to criminal contexts.
verdict: AWARENESS — it states a foundational evidentiary principle about inferences from unexplained silence rather than a rule that directly changes how you gather evidence or charge.

appealFrom: Full Court of the Supreme Court of Victoria
outcome: Special leave to appeal granted to the Crown in each case; appeals allowed; order of the Full Court of the Supreme Court of Victoria discharged; convictions and death sentences of LEE, CLAYTON and ANDREWS restored.
weight: High Court of Australia, five-member bench (LATHAM CJ, McTIERNAN, WEBB, FULLAGAR and KITTO JJ). A long-standing leading authority on the admissibility of confessions, frequently cited. Note that aspects rest on Victorian statute (s 141 of the Evidence Act 1928 (Vict)) and Victorian Standing Orders, and that WA admissibility is now governed by its own statutory regime.
oneLine: The High Court restated the common law test for admitting an accused's statement: it must be voluntary (free choice to speak), and even if voluntary may be excluded only in the court's discretion where it would be unfair to use it — with no onus on the Crown to justify admission.
whatHappened: On the night of 07/11/1949 a man named Kent was killed in a house in Carlton, Melbourne — strangled after being tied up and battered. In the early hours of 08/11/1949 detectives intercepted Jean LEE, Robert CLAYTON and Norman ANDREWS at a Spencer St hotel, searched their rooms, took them to Russell St Police Headquarters at about 4 am, questioned them separately, and charged all three with murder at about 7 am. Each made incriminating statements: CLAYTON admitted being at the house then signed a written statement after caution; LEE, after being shown CLAYTON's statement and confronted with him, admitted attacking Kent and tying his hands; ANDREWS admitted holding Kent and receiving some of the stolen money. There was also strong independent evidence (descriptions, bloodstains, cuts on hands, a torn diary page linking LEE to Kent). All three were convicted before Gavan Duffy J and a jury on 20/03/1950 and sentenced to death. The Full Court of Victoria (majority, O'Bryan J dissenting) on 19/05/1950 quashed the convictions and ordered a new trial. In LEE's case it held her statement was inadmissible; in CLAYTON's and ANDREWS' cases it held their statements were voluntary and could be admitted, but quashed those convictions because the trial judge had miscarried in exercising his discretion. The Crown obtained special leave to appeal to the High Court.
whatHeld: The High Court allowed the Crown appeal and restored all three convictions and sentences. There is really one governing rule: a confessional statement is inadmissible unless the Crown proves it was voluntary — made in the exercise of a free choice to speak or stay silent, and not the product of duress, intimidation, persistent importunity, or sustained or undue pressure. A threat or promise by a person in authority is just one of several things that can rob a statement of voluntariness. Beyond voluntariness, a trial judge has a discretion to reject a voluntary statement where, having regard to the conduct of police and all the circumstances, it would be unfair to use the accused's own words against him. The Court rejected the Full Court's three errors: (1) there is no onus on the Crown to justify exercising the discretion in favour of admission — the statement, once proved voluntary, is prima facie admissible and it is for the accused to bring himself within the exception; (2) the likelihood that police impropriety might produce an untrue admission is a relevant consideration, not an irrelevant one; (3) the test of whether the accused failed "to do justice to his real position" was rejected as dangerously ambiguous — unfairness must arise from the circumstances in which the statement was made, not from whether the statement is true or harmful to the defence run at trial. The Court held the better approach is to ask, as one question, whether it would be unfair to use the accused's statement against him, rather than asking separately whether police acted improperly and then whether rejection follows. The trial judge had correctly found each statement voluntary and had not erred in admitting them.
whatItMeans: This is a foundational confessions authority and it shapes how you take and record statements. The Crown must be able to prove every confessional statement was voluntary — made by genuine free choice — so your conduct and record of the interview must demonstrate that. Voluntariness is broad: it is not just about threats or promises, but covers any wearing down of the will by intimidation, persistent importunity, or sustained or undue pressure. Putting the matters that arouse your suspicion to a suspect and inviting an explanation is legitimate — that is not improper "cross-examination". The case confirms that, in the investigative stage before arrest or charge, you may question persons whether suspected or not, but the moment a person is effectively not free to leave you should consider cautioning. Note this is a Victorian decision turning partly on Victorian legislation and the Chief Commissioner's Standing Orders; in WA you must apply current WA law (the Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA), including recording, caution and right-to-communicate requirements) which now sets stricter, codified obligations. The enduring principle for your casework is that breaching internal rules does not automatically exclude a statement, but the safer course is full compliance and a frank, accurate record of exactly what occurred — because admissibility ultimately turns on voluntariness and fairness, both judged from the circumstances in which the statement was obtained.
verdict: AWARENESS — a landmark statement of the voluntariness and fairness-discretion principles for confessions, but decided under Victorian legislation and superseded for WA practice by the codified regime in the Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA).

outcome: Special leave to appeal refused (per Latham CJ, Dixon and Williams JJ). The voluntariness principle is Dixon J's reasoning.
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: A confession is admissible only if it is voluntary — not obtained by inducement, threat, or a will that has been overborne.
whatHappened: The High Court’s statement of the voluntariness rule governing the admissibility of confessions. (Held at principle level here.)
whatHeld: Per Dixon J (Latham CJ and Williams J agreeing in the result; the Court refused special leave to appeal): a confession is admissible only if voluntary — made in the exercise of a free choice, not the result of duress, intimidation, persistent importunity or sustained or undue pressure that overbears the will ('basal voluntariness'). The famous voluntariness formulation is Dixon J's reasoning; the order was simply to refuse leave.
whatItMeans: Voluntariness is the first hurdle in an interview: caution properly, offer no inducements, and beware sustained or undue pressure that 'overbears' a suspect. If voluntariness fails, the admission is gone, however true. Note there is also a separate discretion to exclude a confession that was voluntary but unfairly or improperly obtained — McDermott itself turned on that discretion (voluntariness was conceded).
verdict: Drives interview conduct: a clean caution, no inducements, no oppression — protect voluntariness.

appealFrom: Court of Criminal Appeal (England)
outcome: Conviction quashed; appeal allowed — the prosecution bears the burden of proving guilt.
weight: House of Lords (UK) — foundational; the presumption-of-innocence cornerstone, applied across the common-law world including WA.
oneLine: The “golden thread” of the criminal law — the prosecution must prove the accused’s guilt; the accused need not prove innocence (subject only to insanity and statutory exceptions).
whatHappened: Reginald WOOLMINGTON, aged 21, was charged with murdering his estranged 17-year-old wife. He claimed the killing was an accident — that he only meant to frighten her into returning to him by threatening suicide with a gun, but it went off. The trial judge directed the jury that, once the killing was proved, it was for WOOLMINGTON to prove it was an accident. He was convicted and sentenced to death; the Court of Criminal Appeal upheld the conviction; the House of Lords quashed it.
whatHeld: Viscount SANKEY LC stated the “golden thread”: it is the duty of the prosecution to prove the prisoner’s guilt, subject only to the defence of insanity and to any statutory exception. If, on the whole of the case, there is a reasonable doubt — raised by either the prosecution’s or the prisoner’s evidence — the prosecution has not made out its case and the accused is entitled to an acquittal. The accused never has to prove innocence.
whatItMeans: This is the cornerstone you build every brief on: the State proves guilt — the suspect proves nothing. Assemble your evidence to discharge that burden to the criminal standard, and never structure a case (or an interview) on the premise that a suspect must explain themselves or account for their innocence. The main carve-out you’ll meet on the job is insanity / unsoundness of mind, which the accused must raise and prove (see Stefanski), plus specific statutory reverse-onus provisions.
verdict: AWARENESS — the foundational statement of the prosecution’s burden; it shapes how you build and think about every brief, even though it lays down no operational procedure.
flags: Full speech not yet in the app — the verbatim ‘golden thread’ ratio is shown; add the complete [1935] AC 462 speech when available.

outcome: Established the rule of practice: put your case to a witness you intend to contradict
weight: UK House of Lords — long-accepted rule of practice in Australian courts
oneLine: If a party intends to contradict a witness, it must put that case to the witness in cross-examination and give them a chance to respond.
whatHappened: The classic statement of the rule of fair cross-examination. (Held at principle level here — on the facts the House of Lords dismissed the appeal, the rule operating against the appellant.)
whatHeld: A party who intends to contradict or impeach a witness must put that case to the witness in cross-examination, so the witness has an opportunity to explain — otherwise it is unfair to later invite the tribunal to disbelieve them.
whatItMeans: In the box the rule cuts both ways: the defence must put their case to you, and your evidence will be tested against it. Your statement and notes must be complete and contemporaneous — a tight record is your armour when your account is challenged.
verdict: Court-craft to understand: complete, contemporaneous notes are your protection when cross-examined.
